findings.topic: PK constraint name convention (claim pkt-a1)

v1.behavior: Synthesizes PK_<Physical>_<KeyCols-by-index-ordinal>, then ConstraintNameNormalizer replaces physical->logical and title-cases single-case tokens, yielding PK_<LogicalTable>_<KeyCols> (fixture pins PK_Customer_Id). Source PK names (OSPRK_*) never pass through.

v2.behavior: IndexNaming.primaryKeyName builds PK_<LogicalKind>_<KeyCols-by-attribute-order> shared by pkDef and the PK backing-index name; budget-fitted. Goldens re-recorded: PK_ScalarGallery_Id, PK_Engagement_Id.

findings.difference: Claim pkt-a1 is STALE: the PK_<Schema>_<Table> shape (PK_dbo_Customer) it describes was replaced by V1's convention — fixed on main 2026-07-16 (WP-8, DECISIONS 27709; packet A1 marked LANDED at SSDT_HANDOFF_REVIEW_PACKET.md:121-131). Conventions now match; goldens byte-pin the V1 shape.
findings.outsystemsRelevant: yes — every table's PK constraint name in the loaded database; the two trunks now agree for native single-column Id PKs
findings.empiricalCheck: Fixture lane: run v1 full-export and v2 publish on the same model; diff 'CONSTRAINT [PK_' lines per table — expect byte equality for single-column PKs. Live lane: deploy v2 bundle to localhost:11433 and SELECT name FROM sys.key_constraints — expect PK_<Entity>_Id shapes.

findings.topic: Residual PK-name deltas after WP-8: composite column order and token casing

v1.behavior: PK name column segment ordered by the primary index's column ordinals (OrderBy(c => c.Ordinal)); each suffix token that is all-upper or all-lower is title-cased by NormalizeToken; column segment uses the emission column name.

v2.behavior: PK name column segment ordered by the kind's ATTRIBUTE order (List.filter IsPrimaryKey over k.Attributes), using the raw logical Attribute.Name with no token-casing normalization; DECISIONS notes this deliberately avoids idx.Columns order.

findings.difference: For composite PKs where index ordinal order differs from attribute declaration order, V1 and V2 emit different segment order (e.g. V1 PK_Order_OrderNo_TenantId vs V2 PK_Order_TenantId_OrderNo); and any all-upper/all-lower attribute or entity name is title-cased by V1 but emitted verbatim by V2 (V1 'PK_User...' from USER vs V2 'PK_USER...'). Also V2 names PK columns from Attribute.Name while the emitted column list uses the column realization — a column rename override can make the PK name cite the pre-rename attribute name.
findings.outsystemsRelevant: conditional — native OutSystems entities have single-column Id PKs with mixed-case names (no delta); external/composite-PK entities and all-caps attribute names hit it
findings.empiricalCheck: Fixture lane: build a model with a composite PK whose index ordinals reverse the attribute order and an ALL-CAPS attribute; run both CLIs and diff the PK constraint line.

findings.topic: FK constraint name synthesis vs source-name honoring (claim pkt-a2)

v1.behavior: When reflection evidence carries a deployed constraint name (RelationshipActualConstraint.Name), it is used as the base then heavily mutated: first token force-replaced with FK (OSFRK_x -> FK_x), owner-column segment appended if absent, 128-cap applied, then physical->logical replacement and token-casing. Multi-column segments supported. Fallback (no evidence) synthesizes FK_<Owner>_<Referenced>_<Cols>.

v2.behavior: Always synthesizes FK_<OwnerTable>_<TargetTable>_<SourceColumn> (single-column), budget-fitted; the in-code comment names honoring Reference.Name as the open reconciliation-plan WP7 remainder. Reference.Name is consumed only by diagnostics and the refactorlog rename detector.

findings.difference: Confirmed open gap, with two refinements the claim and register miss: (1) V2's Reference.Name holds the source ATTRIBUTE logical name (OssysRowsetReader.fs:182 'refName = Name.create attrRow.AttrName'), not a constraint name — honoring it as scoped would emit wrong names; (2) the deployed FK constraint name IS read on the live path (rowsets fk.[name] -> OssysFkRealityRow.FkName, MetadataSnapshotRunner.fs:308,643) but DROPPED at toBundle (MetadataSnapshotRunner.fs:1415-1461 builds ReferenceRow without it), so closing WP7 requires adapter+IR growth, not just an emitter change (matrix row 57 re-open trigger at V1_PARITY_MATRIX.md:116). Note V1's 'honoring' was itself heavy normalization, so for platform-generated OSFRK names both trunks converge on ≈FK_<Owner>_<Target>_<Col>; only hand-curated FK names diverge.
findings.outsystemsRelevant: conditional — platform-named FKs converge; estates with hand-authored FK names on external/hand-managed tables lose the curated stem (upgrade to major if the estate scan finds many non-OSFRK names)
findings.empiricalCheck: Live lane: on localhost:11433 run SELECT name FROM sys.foreign_keys WHERE name NOT LIKE 'OSFRK%' AND name NOT LIKE 'FK_%' to size the curated-name population; then run v1 full-export vs v2 publish and diff emitted FK names for those constraints.

findings.topic: Index name synthesis, authored-name discard, and collision ordinals (claim pkt-a3)

v1.behavior: IndexNameGenerator always synthesizes <UIX|IX>_<Table>_<KeyCols> then normalizes to logical vocabulary; authored/OSIDX names discarded. NO collision handling: two non-PK indexes over the same key columns generate byte-identical names and both emit (deploy fails). Prefix chosen from SOURCE IsUnique while the DDL uniqueness follows the tightening decision, so a decision-demoted index keeps a misleading UIX_ prefix.

v2.behavior: Since 2026-07-01, emittedNames derives IX_/UIX_<Kind>_<Attrs> from typed logical IR; authored/OSIDX names discarded; colliding base names (name derives from key columns only — INCLUDE/filter/options excluded) get deterministic 1-based ordinals in SsKey order, every member numbered; every name budget-fitted; name and CREATE INDEX uniqueness use the same isUnique||EnforceUnique disjunction so prefix and constraint agree structurally.

findings.difference: Claim confirmed: conventions match (both discard authored index names and synthesize from the logical vocabulary), and V2 adds what V1 lacks — collision ordinals (V1 emits duplicate index names on same-key-column families: guaranteed deploy failure) and structural prefix/uniqueness agreement (V1 can emit a UIX_-named non-unique index when a unique decision demotes). V2 ordinal caveat: ordinals renumber when a sibling same-key index is added/removed (cross-release name churn). The stale-doc part of the claim also confirmed: THE_GOLDEN_EMISSION.md:170 still says index-name synthesis is TODO/pass-through-verbatim — contradicted by IndexNaming.fs and the goldens; code wins.
findings.outsystemsRelevant: yes — OSIDX_* platform indexes are ubiquitous; same-key-column index families occur on real estates (duplicate names would break every V1 deploy of such a family)
findings.empiricalCheck: Fixture lane: model two non-unique indexes over the same column; v1 full-export emits two identical CREATE INDEX names (sqlproj build fails), v2 publish emits IX_.._1/_2. Golden already pins the ordinal form (ScalarGallery).

findings.topic: DEFAULT constraint name pass-through and the DF__ auto-name filter (claim pkt-a4)

v1.behavior: Pure pass-through, no synthesis: name emitted iff non-empty (CreateTableStatementBuilder gates on Name.Length>0). NO DF__ filter anywhere — sys.default_constraints.name flows unfiltered through the rowsets SQL (line 423), the model JSON (ModelJsonDeserializer.AttributeDocument.cs:179), and SmoColumnBuilder, so SQL Server auto-names (DF__<table>__<col>__<hex>) from the source environment are baked verbatim into the SSDT project.

v2.behavior: Pass-through only with a reader-side filter: DefaultConstraintName rows starting with 'DF__' (OrdinalIgnoreCase) drop to None so auto-names never enter the IR; authored names (DF_ScalarGallery_Code) emit; unnamed defaults deploy anonymous (WP-9 synthesis planned, packet A4). The model-JSON path carries NO default names at all (OssysJsonReader hard-codes DefaultName=None).

findings.difference: Claim confirmed for V2, but the packet's 'V1 behaved identically' (A4 row) is imprecise: identical only in no-synthesis. The DF__ filter is V2-only — on a real estate where OutSystems-authored defaults get SQL Server auto-names, V1 bakes the source environment's DF__ names into source control (reproduced literally on deploy; wrong on any other environment) while V2 emits them anonymous (fresh auto-name per deploy; perpetual schema-compare noise until WP-9). Additional V2-only gap: model-JSON-sourced runs lose even AUTHORED DF_ names that V1's JSON path preserved (OssysJsonReader.fs:144 vs src/Osm.Json/Deserialization/ModelJsonDeserializer.AttributeDocument.cs:179).
findings.outsystemsRelevant: yes — most deployed OutSystems defaults carry DF__ auto-names, so nearly every defaulted column's emitted bytes differ between trunks on the live path
findings.empiricalCheck: Live lane: against localhost:11433 with an auto-named and a hand-named default, run v1 full-export and v2 publish; grep both outputs for 'DF__' (present only in v1) and 'CONSTRAINT [DF_' (both). Fixture lane: JSON-path run with a named defaultConstraint in model.json — v2 emits it anonymous.

findings.topic: CHECK constraint name pass-through; no CK__ filter on either side

v1.behavior: CHECK names pass through verbatim when present (including SQL Server CK__ auto-names), anonymous otherwise; no synthesis, no filter, no cap.

v2.behavior: Identical pass-through (columnCheckDef maps Name option verbatim; ScriptDomBuild emits ConstraintIdentifier iff Some) — but unlike DEFAULTs there is NO CK__ auto-name filter at the reader (Name.create row.ConstraintName unconditionally), and the ConstraintName also seeds the ColumnCheck SsKey.

findings.difference: Cross-trunk parity on CHECK name pass-through (both bake CK__ auto-names; both emit unnamed CHECKs anonymous — golden pins CK_ScalarGallery_Tally named and an anonymous CHECK at ScalarGallery.sql:9,27). Within V2 there is an unexplained asymmetry: DF__ auto-names are filtered as 'incidental properties of the source instance' but CK__ auto-names — the same phenomenon — pass through into emitted DDL and into SsKey identity, making check identity environment-name-dependent. WP-9 (packet line 1032) plans synthesis for both.
findings.outsystemsRelevant: conditional — CHECK constraints are rare on OutSystems-native tables (hand-added on external/hand-managed tables); when present with auto-names, both trunks bake per-environment names
findings.empiricalCheck: Live lane: add an unnamed CHECK to a warm-SQL table (server auto-names it CK__...), extract with both trunks, confirm both emit the CK__ name verbatim and that v2's catalog.snapshot.json SsKey embeds it.

findings.topic: >128 identifier budget: coverage superset and byte-level hash differences (claim pkt-a6)

v1.behavior: 128-cap with 115-head + '_' + 12-hex SHA-256 exists ONLY for FK names (grep confirms MaxConstraintNameLength/TruncateWithHash appear solely in ForeignKeyNameFactory). Hash hex is UPPERCASE (Convert.ToHexString); head is TrimEnd('_') so results can be <128; the cap runs BEFORE ConstraintNameNormalizer replacement, so physical->logical expansion after truncation can re-exceed 128. PK and index names are never length-checked (latent deploy-failure hole).

v2.behavior: IdentifierBudget.fit: <=128 byte-identical, else exactly 128 = 115-char head + '_' + first 12 LOWERCASE hex of SHA-256(full name); applied as the final op to ALL generated names — PK (SsdtDdlEmitter.fs:221), FK (:308), and every index name (IndexNaming.fs:83,89). Golden pins a live 128-char example whose suffix I verified equals sha256 of the full synthesized name (b94286649f49). Pass-through DF_/CK_/trigger names uncapped (Name.create has no length cap, Catalog.fs:22-33) — WP-11 fit-or-refuse planned (packet:1046).

findings.difference: Claim confirmed and refined: same 115+12 SHAPE, but not byte-faithful in the over-budget case — V1 uppercase hex vs V2 lowercase, V1 TrimEnd('_') vs V2 fixed 115 head, and V1 hashes the pre-normalization logical base while normalizing afterward (a re-expansion >128 leak V2 cannot have since fit is terminal). V2's coverage is a safe superset (V1's uncapped PK/index names are a latent deploy failure on long logical names — packet A6 concurs). Pass-through names uncapped in BOTH; only reachable via hand-authored model JSON since SQL Server caps source names at 128.
findings.outsystemsRelevant: conditional — requires >115-128-char synthesized names (long logical entity+column names); rare but real for external entities; when hit, the two trunks emit different hash-suffix bytes
findings.empiricalCheck: Fixture lane: model an FK whose synthesized name exceeds 128; run both CLIs; compare suffixes (V1 uppercase vs V2 lowercase hex). Deploy the v2 golden Ledger table to localhost:11433 and confirm sys.foreign_keys.name is exactly 128 chars.

findings.topic: Table-name collision tripwire (claim pkt-h7)

v1.behavior: No table-name collision detection either — duplicate effective table names surface only at sqlproj/DacFx build or as file overwrites (PerTableWriter emits per effectiveTableName with no cross-table uniqueness check).
v1.cite: src/Osm.Smo/PerTableWriter.cs:87-185 (no collision check); src/Osm.Pipeline/Orchestration (no table-name uniqueness gate)

v2.behavior: WP-16 landed: tableNameCollisionDiagnostics groups all kinds by emitted (schema, table) and raises one Error per participating kind (emit.ssdt.table.nameCollision), wired into the publish diagnostics next to the FK tripwire.

findings.difference: Claim STALE — fixed on main 2026-07-16 (WP-16; DECISIONS 2026-07-16, packet H7 marked LANDED at SSDT_HANDOFF_REVIEW_PACKET.md:679-683). Refinement: the tripwire is loud but NOT fail-closed — Error diagnostics ride the report and log as Error envelopes while the bundle still writes and the run exits 0 (FullExportRun.fs:40-45 maps Succeeded->0 regardless of diagnostic severity; FullExportRun.fs:96-118 only maps severity to log level). The last-win file overwrite within a module is prevented only in the sense that the operator is told; the DacFx build remains the hard stop.
findings.outsystemsRelevant: yes — same-named entities in different modules all landing in dbo is a routine OutSystems pattern (schema pass-through), making this tripwire load-bearing for real estates
findings.empiricalCheck: Fixture lane: catalog with two modules each containing entity 'Customer'; v2 publish must emit emit.ssdt.table.nameCollision Errors (check transform.diagnostic envelopes) while still writing the bundle and exiting 0; v1 full-export produces colliding files/build failure with no upstream diagnostic.

findings.topic: FK constraint-name collision handling: Error tripwire vs silent dedupe

v1.behavior: processedConstraints HashSet dedupes by evidence KEY (viaAttribute|name|columns) — first wins, rest silently swallowed; distinct constraints whose names collide only AFTER normalization emit duplicate names with no warning (build/deploy failure).

v2.behavior: foreignKeyNameCollisionDiagnostics groups emitted FKs by (schema, fk.Name) and raises one Error per participating reference (emit.ssdt.foreignKey.nameCollision); structurally unreachable for forward references since the name embeds the source column; wired into the publish.

findings.difference: V2 replaces V1's two silent behaviors (silent dedupe of duplicate evidence; silent duplicate-name emission) with a named Error per collision site. Same loud-not-blocking caveat as WP-16 (exit stays 0). This is the naming half of fks-area claim pkt-e5; confirmed in code on both sides.
findings.outsystemsRelevant: yes — the V1 silent dedupe masked the inverse-emission defect class on real CreatedBy/UpdatedBy shapes
findings.empiricalCheck: Covered by DeployableReferenceTests tripwire tests; fixture lane: hand-build a catalog with two references sharing a synthesized name and assert the Error entries appear in the v2 report.

findings.topic: Which constraint names track a table rename/override

v1.behavior: At write time, ResolveConstraintName rewrites PK, FK, and index names by naive case-insensitive substring replacement of BOTH the physical and the logical table name with the effective override name; the referenced-side table token in FK names is never rewritten (only the REFERENCES clause updates); the substring replace can over-fire when the entity name embeds in column tokens (entity User + column UserName -> both replaced).

v2.behavior: TableRename rewrites Kind.Physical only (SsKey and Kind.Name untouched); FK names derive from k.Physical/target.Physical so they track the rename target on BOTH owner and referenced sides; PK and index names derive from the logical Kind.Name and deliberately do NOT change under a rename; derivation is structural (no substring replacement).

findings.difference: Under any configured rename the trunks emit different constraint-name sets: V1 embeds the override name in all three name families (with an over-replacement hazard and a stale referenced-side token); V2 keeps PK/IX names on the logical entity name and updates FK names on both sides. Side discovery: the in-tree V1 rename fixture (tests/Fixtures/emission/edge-case-rename/Modules/AppCore/dbo.CUSTOMER_PORTAL.sql:3 PK_CustomerPortal_Id vs :11 FK_CUSTOMER_PORTAL_City_CityId) contradicts current V1 code — ResolveConstraintName would produce PK_CUSTOMER_PORTAL_Id — so that fixture is stale/hand-edited and unsafe as a byte-parity reference.
findings.outsystemsRelevant: conditional — only when overrides.tableRenames / --rename-table is used; renames are a first-class cutover tool so the divergence will be visible in exactly the migrations that use them
findings.empiricalCheck: Fixture lane: same model + rename Customer->CUSTOMER_PORTAL through both CLIs; diff PK/FK/IX names. Also re-run v1 full-export to regenerate the edge-case-rename fixture and confirm the PK name discrepancy is fixture-staleness, not a code branch.

findings.topic: PK/index-name collisions across tables have no tripwire in V2 (and rename disambiguation does not reach them)

v1.behavior: Same-named logical entities in one schema produce colliding PK_<Logical>_<Cols>/IX_<Logical>_<Cols> names, but because V1 rewrites constraint names to the effective table name, an operator rename that disambiguates the tables ALSO disambiguates the PK/index names.

v2.behavior: PK and index names derive from Kind.Name, which a tableRename never touches; the collision tripwires cover only (schema, table) and (schema, FK-name). Two same-named kinds disambiguated by a rename still emit identical PK_<Kind>_<Cols> and IX_<Kind>_<Cols> names in the same schema — a DacFx duplicate-object failure with no upstream diagnostic.

findings.difference: New finding beyond the claims: V2's collision guard set has a hole exactly in the scenario its own table tripwire pushes operators toward (rename one of two same-named entities; table names now differ; PK/index constraint names still collide schema-wide). V1's substring-rewrite approach, whatever its other flaws, made constraint names follow the disambiguation.
findings.outsystemsRelevant: conditional — requires two same-named entities in one target schema plus a rename-based disambiguation; plausible for multi-module estates consolidating into dbo
findings.empiricalCheck: Fixture lane: two modules each with entity Customer, rename one to CustomerB via overrides.tableRenames; v2 publish emits PK_Customer_Id twice in dbo with no Error diagnostic; DacFx build (or deploy to localhost:11433) fails with duplicate object.

findings.topic: Mixed name vocabulary under physical-form rename pins / logical-emission Disabled

v1.behavior: One vocabulary per run: all names (table, PK, FK, IX) converge on the effective table name via GetEffectiveTableName + ResolveConstraintName, whether that is logical (default) or an override target.

v2.behavior: A physical-form tableRename pins the kind out of LogicalTableEmission (S6.3), and Disabled mode skips substitution for all kinds — in both cases k.Physical stays physical/authored while k.Name stays logical. FK names then read k.Physical (physical vocabulary) while PK/index names read k.Name (logical vocabulary) on the same table.

findings.difference: In V2's physical-name emission modes a single table carries CREATE TABLE [dbo].[OSUSR_ABC_CUSTOMER] with FK_OSUSR_ABC_CUSTOMER_* but PK_Customer_Id and IX_Customer_* — two vocabularies in one object, which V1 never produces. The Disabled mode is explicitly advertised for 'V1-parity/diagnostic reasons' (LogicalTableEmission.fs:52-58) yet does not reproduce V1's one-vocabulary output.
findings.outsystemsRelevant: conditional — only physical-pin renames or the logical-emission off-switch; the default chain is unaffected
findings.empiricalCheck: Fixture lane: publish with a physical-form tableRename (or LogicalTableEmission Disabled) and grep the pinned table's file for PK_/IX_ (logical) vs FK_/table (physical) tokens.

findings.topic: V2 index-name ordinal renumbering across releases

v1.behavior: No ordinal scheme exists (colliding families are simply broken), so no churn axis.

v2.behavior: Ordinals assign per colliding group in SsKey order at emit time; adding or removing one member of a same-key-column family renumbers its siblings (IX_T_Col_2 can become IX_T_Col_1 in the next publish), a documented characteristic with a named re-open trigger for an overrides.indexNames axis.

findings.difference: Deterministic within a catalog but unstable across catalog edits: index drop/recreate churn in DacFx deployments whenever a colliding family changes shape. Not present in V1 only because V1's alternative was outright duplicate names.
findings.outsystemsRelevant: conditional — only same-key-column index families; harmless (indexes rebuild) but shows as schema-compare noise on incremental deploys
findings.empiricalCheck: Fixture lane: publish, remove one of three same-key indexes from the model, publish again, diff index names (renumbering visible in the two bundles).

findings.topic: Constraint-name prefix configurability (PK/UIX/IX/FK)

v1.behavior: IndexNamingOptions record carries configurable prefixes with WithIndexNaming, but nothing binds it from CLI/config — only Default (PK/UIX/IX/FK) is ever used in production paths.

v2.behavior: Prefixes are hard-coded string literals in the naming derivations; no knob exists or is planned.

findings.difference: Effective parity: both trunks emit fixed PK_/UIX_/IX_/FK_ prefixes; V1's knob is dead code (capability on paper only).
findings.outsystemsRelevant: no — no operator surface reaches the V1 knob, so no observable difference for any estate
findings.empiricalCheck: Static only: grep V1 config binding (Osm.Json/Osm.Cli) for IndexNaming — absent; both outputs always show the four fixed prefixes.

findings.topic: Trigger name pass-through (uncapped, unrenamed)

v1.behavior: Trigger names emit verbatim (comment banner + DISABLE TRIGGER use trigger.Name); no cap; body table-references rewritten but the trigger NAME itself never renamed.

v2.behavior: Trigger names emit verbatim via Name.value t.Name in the banner comment and CREATE TRIGGER; no cap (Name.create blank-check only).

findings.difference: Parity on the NAME axis (pass-through, uncapped both) — the trigger BODY physical-reference issue is the renames dimension (packet H2), not naming.
findings.outsystemsRelevant: conditional — hand-authored triggers only
findings.empiricalCheck: Fixture lane: both CLIs on a model with a trigger; diff the trigger name tokens (equal).

findings.topic: Untrusted-FK emission mechanics and deployed end state

v1.behavior: NOCHECK FKs are deferred out of CREATE TABLE and emitted as 'ALTER TABLE [s].[t]  WITH NOCHECK ADD CONSTRAINT ... FOREIGN KEY ...' (note double space) followed unconditionally by 'ALTER TABLE ... NOCHECK CONSTRAINT [fk];' which DISABLES the constraint — deployed end state is_disabled=1 (no enforcement of new rows). Trigger: source FkReality.IsNoCheck OR policy ScriptWithNoCheck && allowNoCheckCreation.

v2.behavior: FK stays INLINE in CREATE TABLE (created trusted), then a two-step pair: 'ALTER TABLE ... NOCHECK CONSTRAINT [fk]' (disable→untrusted) then 'ALTER TABLE ... WITH NOCHECK CHECK CONSTRAINT [fk]' (re-enable skipping validation) — end state is_not_trusted=1, is_disabled=0, verified against SQL Server per 6.A.6. Trigger: Reference.IsConstraintTrusted=false (live-populated since WP-1a) OR overlay.NoCheckFk.

findings.difference: For the same untrusted-source FK the two tools deploy different constraint states: v1 leaves it DISABLED (new orphan rows accepted, is_disabled=1); v2 leaves it ENABLED-UNTRUSTED (new rows enforced, existing unvalidated, is_disabled=0) — v2 reproduces the source estate's actual WITH-NOCHECK state, v1 loosens it. Also v1's stale curated fixture (edge-case-untrusted/Ops/dbo.JobRun.sql:12-13) lacks the second NOCHECK statement current code always emits — the fixture is not asserted by any test and has drifted from v1 code.
findings.outsystemsRelevant: conditional — OutSystems never creates untrusted FKs, but DBA-added WITH NOCHECK constraints and the policy ScriptWithNoCheck path (orphan data) hit this on real estates
findings.empiricalCheck: live lane: deploy both outputs for a table whose source FK has is_not_trusted=1; SELECT name,is_disabled,is_not_trusted FROM sys.foreign_keys on localhost:11433 — expect v1 (1,1) vs v2 (0,1); also try inserting an orphan row (v1 accepts, v2 rejects)

findings.topic: CREATE SCHEMA emission for non-dbo estates (G6, PR #671)

v1.behavior: No CREATE SCHEMA emission anywhere (grep of src/ finds none); per-table files reference [billing].[...] etc. and the classic sqlproj globs only Modules\**\*.sql — a non-dbo estate's dacpac build hits unresolved-schema references with no schema object supplied.

v2.behavior: Every distinct non-dbo schema (kinds + sequences, case-insensitive-deduped, ordinal-sorted) emits Statement.CreateSchema FIRST in the stream and as per-schema bundle files Schemas/<name>.sql ('CREATE SCHEMA [audit]' + framed GO), riding the SDK Build glob; dbo never emitted; DacFx-proven per PR #671.
v2.cite: sidecar/projection/src/Projection.Targets.SSDT/SsdtDdlEmitter.fs:601-630,984-987; ScriptDomBuild.fs:2679-2682; SsdtBundle.fs:46-61; Pipeline.fs:592-593; golden Schemas/audit.sql:1 and stream.sql:1-3

findings.difference: v2 makes a non-dbo estate self-contained and buildable (Schemas/<schema>.sql, note: folder is 'Schemas/', not 'Security/'); v1's bundle omits schema objects entirely, so external-entity schemas (billing., audit., ...) require hand-authored schema scripts before its sqlproj/dacpac path builds.
findings.outsystemsRelevant: yes — external entities and extension databases commonly live outside dbo in real estates
findings.empiricalCheck: fixture lane: run v1 full-export on the edge-case model (billing schema) and dotnet-build the emitted sqlproj — expect SQL71501 unresolved schema reference; v2 publish of a non-dbo catalog then dotnet build ProjectionCatalog.sqlproj succeeds

findings.topic: Trigger emission batching and disable statement

v1.behavior: Per trigger, ONE joined string: '-- Trigger: <physName> (disabled: x)' + raw definition (with regex-style physical→effective identifier rewrites) + when disabled 'ALTER TABLE ... DISABLE TRIGGER [physName];' — all inside a single GO batch, and the DISABLE references the UN-rewritten physical trigger name while the CREATE's name got rewritten by the table-name substring replace (JobRun fixture creates [TR_JobRun_AUDIT] but disables [TR_OSUSR_XYZ_JOBRUN_AUDIT]).

v2.behavior: Per trigger, THREE separate statements each in its own GO batch: Comment metadata line, CreateTrigger (definition parsed via TSql160Parser and re-rendered through ScriptDom; parse failure → in-band TriggerBodyUnparsedDropped marker comment on text path, refusal on dacpac path), then AlterTableDisableTrigger built from typed AST using the same Trigger.Name.

findings.difference: v1 puts CREATE TRIGGER and a following ALTER in the same batch — invalid T-SQL batching that its own per-file TSql150 parse validation (SsdtSqlValidator.cs:95-99) should flag — and its disable statement can name a nonexistent trigger under rename; v2's per-statement GO framing is deploy-valid and name-consistent, at the cost of re-formatting the trigger body through ScriptDom (byte-level drift from source definition text).
findings.outsystemsRelevant: conditional — only estates with table triggers (audit/CDC-style DBA additions); OutSystems itself creates few
findings.empiricalCheck: fixture lane: run v1 full-export on a model with a disabled trigger and check the validation-step report for a parse error on that file; deploy v2 output with a disabled trigger and confirm sys.triggers.is_disabled=1

findings.topic: CLUSTERED keyword on composite (table-level) PK

v1.behavior: Composite PK table constraint sets Clustered=true → emits 'PRIMARY KEY CLUSTERED ([A], [B])'; single-column inline PK also CLUSTERED.

v2.behavior: Composite PK table constraint leaves ScriptDom Clustered unset → emits 'PRIMARY KEY ([A], [B])' without CLUSTERED; single-column inline PK sets Clustered=Nullable(true) → 'PRIMARY KEY CLUSTERED'.

findings.difference: v2 omits CLUSTERED on composite PKs while v1 states it; SQL Server defaults a PK to clustered so the deployed table is identical — declaratively asymmetric only (packet B1 wording confirmed).
findings.outsystemsRelevant: conditional — composite PKs only on external/static junction tables; deployed semantics identical
findings.empiricalCheck: deploy both and compare sys.indexes.type_desc for the PK (both CLUSTERED); textual diff of the two files shows the keyword delta

findings.topic: sp_addextendedproperty statement style

v1.behavior: Hand-built compact form: 'EXEC sys.sp_addextendedproperty @name=N'MS_Description', @value=N'...',' with no spaces around '=', no space after the comma inside a level pair ('@level0type=N'SCHEMA',@level0name=N'dbo','), terminating semicolon; only MS_Description properties exist.

v2.behavior: Typed ScriptDom ExecuteStatement renders 'EXECUTE [sys].[sp_addextendedproperty] @name = N'...', @value = N'...',' (spaces around '=', space after commas, bracketed proc, no semicolon), wrapped at @level0/@level1/@level2 boundaries with 4-space continuation by the formatter — the same wrap points as v1.

findings.difference: EXEC vs EXECUTE [sys].[...], '=' spacing, comma spacing, and trailing ';' differ byte-wise; wrap structure is identical. This is the documented accepted deviation (packet B4). The ADMIRE claim that v2's EXEC was 'still single-line' is STALE — formatExtendedPropertyExec landed the multi-line wrap.
findings.outsystemsRelevant: yes — every table/column description emits one of these; diff tooling comparing v1 vs v2 trees must normalize
findings.empiricalCheck: fixture lane: diff v1 fixture dbo.Customer.sql lines 34-36 against a v2 emission of the same description; both deploy identical sys.extended_properties rows

findings.topic: Vendor identity extended properties (H6)

v1.behavior: Emits only MS_Description extended properties (table/column/index-description); no vendor identity metadata of any kind.

v2.behavior: Additionally emits Projection.LogicalName + Projection.SsKey per TABLE and per COLUMN (renamed from V2.* per C1), plus authored ExtendedProperties and module SCHEMA-level properties; gated by emission.identityAnnotations (default true; false is a named downgrade emitting emission.identityAnnotations.omitted and degrading rename round-trips).

findings.difference: A v2-deployed database carries 2 extra extended properties per table and per column that a v1 deployment lacks; schema-compare tools (DacFx, sys.extended_properties audits) will report them as drift between v1- and v2-managed environments; pre-rename environments may still carry legacy V2.*-named rows (environment state, not code).
findings.outsystemsRelevant: yes — visible in any estate cutover comparison and in DBA metadata audits
findings.empiricalCheck: deploy v2 bundle, then SELECT name FROM sys.extended_properties WHERE name LIKE 'Projection.%' — expect 2 per table + 2 per column; re-run with emission.identityAnnotations=false — expect zero plus the named diagnostic in NDJSON

findings.topic: ON UPDATE referential action

v1.behavior: SmoForeignKeyDefinition has DeleteAction only — no UpdateAction field exists; ScriptDom UpdateAction never set, so a source FK's ON UPDATE CASCADE/SET NULL is silently dropped (deploys as NO ACTION). SetDefault also unmapped for the inline path (MapDeleteAction folds it to NoAction).

v2.behavior: ForeignKeyDef.OnUpdate : ReferenceActionSql option threads to ScriptDom UpdateAction; ladder renders 'ON DELETE NO ACTION / ON UPDATE CASCADE' pairs (backfill rule shared with v1's formatter).

findings.difference: v2 preserves ON UPDATE actions end-to-end; v1 cannot represent them, so a DBA-added ON UPDATE CASCADE FK loses its update rule on v1 redeploy (loaded-database semantics differ on UPDATE of referenced keys).
findings.outsystemsRelevant: conditional — OutSystems-generated FKs never carry ON UPDATE; DBA-added ones can
findings.empiricalCheck: live lane: source table with ON UPDATE CASCADE FK; deploy both; compare sys.foreign_keys.update_referential_action (v1=0, v2=1)

findings.topic: CREATE TABLE column ordering

v1.behavior: Emits columns in model attribute order verbatim — no reordering (proven by Generate_preserves_column_order_from_model; SmoModelFactory sorts modules/tables but never columns).

v2.behavior: CanonicalizeIdentity re-sorts attributes: PK first, then authored Service-Studio Order (ossys_Entity_Attr.Order_Num / JSON 'order'), then SsKey tiebreak (WP8/NM-72); all emitters iterate that canonical list order.

findings.difference: When the model's attribute order does not lead with the PK (or ties need breaking), v2's deployed column ordinals differ from v1's; SELECT *, ordinal-based INSERTs, and schema comparisons see different column positions.
findings.outsystemsRelevant: conditional — OutSystems always authors Id first so orders normally coincide; divergence appears only for hand-built/edge catalogs
findings.empiricalCheck: fixture lane: model with PK authored second; compare column ordinals of the two CREATE TABLEs and sys.columns.column_id after deploy

findings.topic: CHECK-body parse-failure fallback

v1.behavior: ParsePredicate returns null on TSql150 parse errors and the CHECK constraint is silently skipped — no constraint object deploys, no diagnostic.

v2.behavior: TSql160 parse failure emits a degenerate tautology CHECK ('<raw text>' = '<raw text>') so a named constraint object still deploys but enforces nothing; doc comment defers a Diagnostics warning.

findings.difference: Both lose enforcement on unparseable CHECK bodies but differently: v1 drops the object silently (schema-compare shows missing constraint), v2 deploys a vacuous constraint whose definition text mismatches source (schema-compare shows body drift). pkt-a5's second half confirmed; neither refuses.
findings.outsystemsRelevant: conditional — real V1-sourced CHECK definitions parse cleanly; exotic DBA expressions could hit it
findings.empiricalCheck: fixture lane: catalog with an intentionally unparseable ColumnCheck.Definition; inspect emitted file for CHECK ('...' = '...') in v2 and absence in v1

findings.topic: Untrusted CHECK constraints (IsNotTrusted)

v1.behavior: SmoCheckConstraintDefinition carries IsNotTrusted but BuildColumnDefinition never reads it — untrusted source CHECK deploys trusted/enforced; no NOCHECK step exists for checks.

v2.behavior: ColumnCheckDef.IsNotTrusted is carried (live rowset extracts ck.is_not_trusted) but checkConstraint explicitly documents not consuming it; only FKs get the NOCHECK two-step.

findings.difference: pkt-a5's framing as a v2-regression is REFUTED as a v1-vs-v2 delta: BOTH tools deploy an untrusted source CHECK as trusted (and both would fail deploy if existing data violates it). It is a shared fidelity gap, not a regression from v1.
findings.outsystemsRelevant: conditional — CHECKs appear on external/DBA-touched tables; an untrusted one with violating rows breaks deploy in both tools identically
findings.empiricalCheck: live lane: source CHECK with is_not_trusted=1 over violating rows; both emitted scripts fail at CREATE TABLE/seed time the same way

findings.topic: Computed columns and PERSISTED

v1.behavior: Computed columns emit '[col] AS <expr>' (DataType null, no nullability/identity); IsPersisted never set — PERSISTED always lost; extraction SQL has no is_persisted column.

v2.behavior: Emit side supports PERSISTED (col.IsPersisted <- true when ComputedColumnConfig.IsPersisted) but the live rowset/reader never extracts is_persisted (only IsComputed + ComputedDefinition), so live-path IR can't carry it.

findings.difference: pkt-c10 refined: PERSISTED is lost end-to-end in BOTH tools on the live path (shared gap); v2 alone has emit-side support so closing it is an adapter-only change; a persisted computed column redeploys non-persisted (index-on-computed-column and performance semantics change) in both.
findings.outsystemsRelevant: conditional — computed columns exist only on external/DBA tables in OutSystems estates
findings.empiricalCheck: live lane: source persisted computed column; both outputs deploy is_persisted=0 (sys.computed_columns)

findings.topic: CREATE SEQUENCE emission reachability

v1.behavior: No sequence modeling or emission anywhere; extraction SQL never reads sys.sequences.

v2.behavior: CreateSequence statement + buildCreateSequence emit AS/START/INCREMENT/MIN/MAX/CYCLE/CACHE, ordered before tables; Catalog.Sequences is populated by the model-JSON adapter (OssysJsonReader slice δ) but NOT by the live OssysSql rowset path (no sequence extraction).

findings.difference: pkt-c10's 'sequences absent from model-sourced runs' is INVERTED: the model-JSON path populates sequences; it is the LIVE rowset path that cannot see them. Net: v2 is a strict superset of v1 (which has nothing), but live-path estates still lose sequences in both.
findings.outsystemsRelevant: conditional — sequences only exist as DBA additions; a DEFAULT referencing NEXT VALUE FOR then fails deploy in both live paths
findings.empiricalCheck: fixture lane: model JSON with a sequence → v2 emits CREATE SEQUENCE before tables, v1 emits nothing; live lane against a DB with a sequence → neither extracts it

findings.topic: COLLATE clause suppression rules

v1.behavior: Two suppressions: extraction NULLIFs collation equal to the DATABASE default (shared SQL), and emission additionally skips the hard-coded 'Latin1_General_CI_AI' even when it is NOT the database default.

v2.behavior: Only the extraction-level database-default NULLIF (same rowset text); emission re-states any surviving collation verbatim (COLLATE <name>, unquoted identifier).

findings.difference: A column explicitly collated Latin1_General_CI_AI in a database whose default differs: v1 silently drops the clause (column falls back to database default on deploy — semantic change); v2 preserves it. Identical behavior whenever the DB default IS Latin1_General_CI_AI (the OutSystems norm).
findings.outsystemsRelevant: conditional — bites only estates hosted on non-OutSystems-default collation servers
findings.empiricalCheck: live lane: DB with default SQL_Latin1_General_CP1_CI_AS and one column collated Latin1_General_CI_AI; v1 file lacks COLLATE, v2 file carries it

findings.topic: Newline bytes, EOF shape, platform determinism

v1.behavior: Environment.NewLine throughout (AppendLine in builders + writer) — LF on Linux, CRLF on Windows; file ends with exactly one trailing newline; UTF-8 no BOM.

v2.behavior: LF pinned: ScriptDom output CRLF-collapsed (pinNewlines), formatter hard-codes '\n' and strips stray CR; per-table files end with statement newline + one extra blank line (out.Append('\n')); golden tests enforce no-CRLF + trailing-newline. Caveat: Render.toSql's Blank/Comment/GO arms use sb.AppendLine (Environment.NewLine) — normalized by the formatter only in the default Enabled mode, so renderConstraintsElegant=false on a Windows host could emit mixed CRLF/LF GO framing.

findings.difference: v2 is platform-byte-deterministic (LF always) where v1 is host-dependent (CRLF on Windows); v2 files carry one extra trailing blank line v1 files lack; v2's Disabled-mode Windows edge is an un-pinned residue.
findings.outsystemsRelevant: yes — byte-diffs between machines and against source control matter for the R6 canary and operator diffs

findings.topic: Terminal GO: per-table vs stream.sql vs Schemas files

v1.behavior: GO only BETWEEN statements, never terminal, in the only artifact shape (per-table files); no stream/schema artifacts exist.

v2.behavior: Per-table files: GO between only (BatchSeparator interleaved, golden-enforced never-trailing); stream.sql and Schemas/<name>.sql: separator appended AFTER every statement → terminal GO (deliberate asymmetry).
v2.cite:
sidecar/projection/src/Projection.Targets.SSDT/SsdtDdlEmitter.fs:865-869 vs 626-630,980-987; golden stream.sql tail 'GO


' and Schemas/audit.sql; GoldenEmissionTests.fs:228-231 (scoped to Modules/ only)

findings.difference: pkt-b3 confirmed at byte level; the per-table GO-between form matches v1 exactly; the terminal-GO forms exist only for v2-only artifacts so no v1 counterpart diverges.
findings.outsystemsRelevant: yes — the per-table files are the promoted Azure DevOps surface; framing parity holds where it matters
findings.empiricalCheck: grep -c '^GO$' + tail-byte inspection across a v2 bundle: Modules/** never ends in GO, stream.sql and Schemas/* do

findings.topic: Statement terminators in DDL files

v1.behavior: ScriptDom-generated DDL carries no ';' (despite IncludeSemicolons=true — single-statement GenerateScript emits none); hand-built statements DO end with ';' (sp_addextendedproperty, ALTER ... NOCHECK CONSTRAINT, ALTER ... DISABLE TRIGGER).

v2.behavior: No semicolons anywhere in DDL files (every statement typed-AST rendered, none hand-built); data lanes append ';' per statement by design.

findings.difference: Byte delta on exactly the statements v1 hand-builds (EXEC EP, NOCHECK ALTERs, trigger-disable); execution semantics unchanged.
findings.outsystemsRelevant: yes — affects textual diffs of the same estate emitted by both tools
findings.empiricalCheck: grep ';$' over both trees: v1 hits on EXEC/ALTER lines only, v2 zero in Modules/

findings.topic: Multi-column CHECK constraint placement

v1.behavior: All CHECKs ride SmoColumnDefinition.CheckConstraints and emit inline under the carrying column (there is no table-constraint CHECK slot), including expressions referencing other columns.

v2.behavior: Structural attachment: bracketed-token match against column identifiers — exactly one distinct referenced column ⇒ inline beneath it; multi-column or unresolvable ⇒ table-level two-line ladder.

findings.difference: A CHECK referencing two columns sits under one column in v1 but at table level in v2 — same deployed constraint, different declaration position/bytes.
findings.outsystemsRelevant: conditional — multi-column CHECKs only on DBA-touched tables
findings.empiricalCheck: fixture lane: two-column CHECK; diff placement in the two CREATE TABLEs; sys.check_constraints identical after deploy

findings.topic: Extended-property binding for PK-index descriptions

v1.behavior: Index-description EP uses @level2type=N'CONSTRAINT' when the index is the PK, N'INDEX' otherwise.

v2.behavior: IndexProperty owner always emits @level2type=N'INDEX', including for PK-backing indexes (owner follows the emitted index/constraint name).

findings.difference: For a described PK index the property attaches to a different metadata class (constraint object vs index) — sys.extended_properties rows differ in class/major-minor ids; retrieval queries keyed on one binding miss the other.
findings.outsystemsRelevant: conditional — index descriptions are rare; only described PK indexes diverge
findings.empiricalCheck: deploy both for a PK index with a description; compare sys.extended_properties.class_desc for the row

findings.topic: Per-table /* ... */ header banner

v1.behavior: Opt-in feature: '/*\n    Label: value...\n*/' block (Source/Profile/Decisions/Fingerprint + LogicalName/Module/RenamedFrom items, label-sorted) prepended when emission.emitTableHeaders=true — but the shipped default config and the domain default are FALSE, so default full-export emits no banner.

v2.behavior: No header capability at all; the omission is the named tolerance HeaderCommentsOmitted (AcceptedFaithful) and Provenance is hard-emptied.

findings.difference: pkt-b7 refined: byte parity holds for DEFAULT runs on both sides (no banner either way); what v2 lacks is only the opt-in knob. An operator who had enabled emitTableHeaders loses the provenance banner on cutover.
findings.outsystemsRelevant: conditional — only if the incumbent pipeline enabled the flag
findings.empiricalCheck: run v1 full-export with default config and confirm no '/*' prefix in Modules files (matching v2); rerun with emitTableHeaders=true to see the v1-only banner

findings.topic: emission.renderConstraintsElegant bisect lever (B2)

v1.behavior: No equivalent single knob; nearest is SmoFormatOptions.NormalizeWhitespace=false which skips CreateTableFormatter/ladder (leaving raw ScriptDom text) but is not config-exposed for full-export.

v2.behavior: Config axis emission.renderConstraintsElegant (default true) threads to ConstraintFormatter.Mode; false bypasses the whole ladder/EXEC-wrap post-processor leaving compact ScriptDom output with raw explicit ON DELETE NO ACTION (FK builder always sets DeleteAction); registered as OperatorIntent Emission metadata.

findings.difference: pkt-b2 confirmed in current code: default-true, all-or-nothing text post-processor over pinned ScriptDom 170.23.0, NO-ACTION backfill/drop normalization inside it, Disabled = compact + explicit ON DELETE NO ACTION.
findings.outsystemsRelevant: yes — the documented regression-bisect lever for cutover triage
findings.empiricalCheck: publish twice toggling emission.renderConstraintsElegant and diff one FK-bearing table: Enabled = ladder without NO ACTION pair; Disabled = one-line constraint with 'ON DELETE NO ACTION'

findings.topic: Bare-table emission variants (Modules.Bare)

v1.behavior: TableEmissionMode FullOnly/BareOnly/Both; Both writes a parallel Modules.Bare tree of constraint-less tables (EmitBareTableOnly skips defaults/checks/indexes/EPs/triggers); default FullOnly.

v2.behavior: No bare-variant concept; one full emission shape per kind.

findings.difference: Operators using bare variants (bulk-load staging / DMM-compare workflows) lose the mode on cutover; default runs are unaffected.
findings.outsystemsRelevant: conditional — only if the estate's workflow used emitTableMode!=FullOnly
findings.empiricalCheck: v1 full-export with emitTableMode=Both produces Modules.Bare/**; no v2 config key reaches an equivalent

findings.topic: Identifier-quote strategy configurability

v1.behavior: IdentifierQuoteStrategy SquareBracket|DoubleQuote|None selectable via SmoFormatOptions (bracket default).

v2.behavior: Square-bracket quoting hard-pinned via ScriptDom Identifier.EncodeIdentifier / QuoteType.SquareBracket; no alternative strategy exists.

findings.difference: v1's non-bracket quote modes have no v2 counterpart; irrelevant for SSDT output (brackets are the convention) but a config surface that silently disappears.
findings.outsystemsRelevant: no — SSDT consumers expect brackets; the knob is effectively unused
findings.empiricalCheck: none needed beyond code cites; a v1 run with DoubleQuote demonstrates the mode v2 lacks

findings.topic: Stale emitter comment vs WP-1a reality

v1.behavior: n/a (v1 side of the trust wiring is SmoForeignKeyBuilder, live and consistent).

v2.behavior: SsdtDdlEmitter still says 'Today no adapter populates IsConstraintTrusted = false; this seam is structurally positioned for the rowset-path JOIN slice' — but MetadataSnapshotRunner already populates it from #FkReality.IsNoCheck (WP-1a landed), and the golden emits the NOCHECK pair.

findings.difference: Code comment contradicts current code — the untrusted-FK path IS live-reachable; per the audit rules the contradiction is itself a finding (doc drift inside source).
findings.outsystemsRelevant: yes — a reviewer trusting the comment would mis-assess live-path NOCHECK coverage
findings.empiricalCheck: live lane against a DB with an untrusted FK: v2 bundle contains the two ALTER statements despite the comment

findings.topic: Emission architecture: typed-AST coverage

v1.behavior: Hybrid: core CREATE TABLE/INDEX via ScriptDom Sql150ScriptGenerator, but heavy string post-processing (CreateTableFormatter + ConstraintFormatter + StatementBatchFormatter over split lines) plus fully hand-built string statements (WITH NOCHECK ADD ALTERs, NOCHECK CONSTRAINT, sp_addextendedproperty, trigger rewrite via case-insensitive substring replace, DISABLE TRIGGER).

v2.behavior: Every SQL-bearing statement builds a typed ScriptDom AST (closed-DU dispatch in buildStatement) rendered by pinned Sql160ScriptGenerator; the ONE sanctioned text post-processor is ConstraintFormatter (LINT-ALLOW-FILE, operator-blessed aesthetics); GO is the sole literal (no AST equivalent).

findings.difference: dec-typed-ast-canonical refined: v1 is not purely string-built (it also uses ScriptDom) but v2 eliminates all hand-composed SQL statements; consequence is structural (grammar guaranteed by generator) rather than byte-visible where shapes agree.
findings.outsystemsRelevant: yes — affects defect surface for exotic identifiers (embedded ']', quotes) in hand-built v1 statements
findings.empiricalCheck: fixture lane: table name containing ']' — v1's hand-built ALTER escapes via QuoteIdentifier (ok) but trigger substring-rewrite can corrupt; v2 AST-quotes uniformly

findings.topic: Platform-auto (OSIDX) index keep/prune default

v1.behavior: Shipped default PRUNES non-unique platform-auto indexes: includePlatformAutoIndexes=false in both the code default and the shipped config; SmoIndexBuilder skips IsPlatformAuto && !IsUnique when the flag is off. V1 fixture output confirms OSIDX_JOBRUN_CREATEDON absent.

v2.behavior: Default KEEPS platform-auto indexes: IncludePlatformAutoIndexes=true in both the config binder default and EmissionPolicy default; prune only when operator sets emission.includePlatformAutoIndexes=false (post-chain seam Policy.filterPlatformAutoIndexes). The Policy doc-comment wrongly says 'true (V1 default)'.

findings.difference: Opposite shipped defaults: a default V1 full-export omits every non-unique OSIDX_% index; a default V2 publish keeps them (renamed IX_<Kind>_<Attrs>). V2's default emitted index set is a strict superset of V1's on any real estate. V2's in-code claim that keep=V1-default is false (packet D2 has the correct history); Policy.fs:207 doc-comment contradicts V1 code.
findings.outsystemsRelevant: yes — every OutSystems estate carries OSIDX_% auto indexes (typically the only indexes on FK columns); the default-output index sets differ on essentially every table with an FK
findings.empiricalCheck: Fixture lane: run v1 full-export with config/default-tightening.json and v2 publish with default projection.json over the same edge-case model; diff CREATE INDEX counts (v2 shows the renamed OSIDX index, v1 does not). Live lane: seed ossys-edge-case.seed.sql on localhost:11433 (contains OSIDX_JOBRUN_CREATEDON) and compare both outputs.

findings.topic: Prune scope: unique platform-auto indexes

v1.behavior: Prune keeps platform-auto UNIQUE indexes: the skip predicate is (!IncludePlatformAutoIndexes && IsPlatformAuto && !IsUnique) — uniqueness enforcement survives pruning. Same predicate mirrored in EmissionCoverageCalculator.

v2.behavior: Prune removes ALL IsPlatformAuto=true indexes regardless of uniqueness: List.filter (fun i -> not i.IsPlatformAuto) with no uniqueness carve-out.

findings.difference: Under prune configuration, V2 silently drops platform-auto UNIQUE indexes that V1 preserved — a uniqueness constraint disappears from the deployed schema with no diagnostic. Packet D2 correctly records this divergence but it remains un-remediated in code.
findings.outsystemsRelevant: conditional — only when the operator sets includePlatformAutoIndexes=false (V1's old default posture); OutSystems does create unique OSIDX indexes for unique attributes, so real uniqueness loss is plausible under that config
findings.empiricalCheck: Fixture lane: catalog with a unique OSIDX_% index + includePlatformAutoIndexes=false; v1 emits CREATE UNIQUE INDEX, v2 emits nothing (compare against Golden/pruned-platform-auto which only exercises a non-unique OSIDX).

findings.topic: OSIDX detection on V2's live/rowset path

v1.behavior: isPlatformAuto is computed in the extraction SQL (IndexName LIKE 'OSIDX\_%') inside the #IdxJson aggregation, flows into osm_model.json, and is consumed by the JSON deserializer — so the keep/prune flag works end-to-end on live extractions.

v2.behavior: The live path reads the structured #AllIdx rowset, whose IndexRow has no IsPlatformAuto field and no OSIDX heuristic anywhere in F# code; OssysRowsetReader leaves Index.IsPlatformAuto at its create-default false ('rowset path doesn't surface it'). The only OSIDX_% heuristic in the sidecar lives in the carbon-copied SQL's #IdxJson, which the live path skips via @SkipJsonRowsets. Only the model-JSON path populates the flag.

findings.difference: On V2's flagship live-SQL path, emission.includePlatformAutoIndexes=false is a silent no-op: nothing is ever flagged, so nothing prunes. This refutes packet D2's 'OSIDX_% name-prefix heuristic on the live path' and makes the D2 knob path-dependent (works only when sourcing from osm_model.json). The golden pruned-platform-auto scenario is a hand-built catalog and cannot catch this.
findings.outsystemsRelevant: yes if the operator wants V1-parity pruning on a live cutover extraction — the knob they would reach for does nothing, with no diagnostic
findings.empiricalCheck: Live lane against localhost:11433: seed OSIDX_JOBRUN_CREATEDON (ossys-edge-case.seed.sql:214), run projection publish with emission.includePlatformAutoIndexes=false from model.source=live; the index still emits (as IX_JobRun_CreatedOn). Repeat with model-JSON source: it prunes.

findings.topic: Uniqueness tightening polarity: demotion vs promotion

v1.behavior: Tightening always runs; only model-unique indexes are evaluated (non-unique short-circuits to no-op), and the mode matrix can DEMOTE: a declared-unique index emits NON-unique under PolicyDisabled, DuplicatesWithoutPhysicalReality, or (Cautious/EvidenceGated) CleanWithoutEvidence — but physically-backed unique (OnDisk.Kind PK/UQ/UIX or profile IsUniqueKey) always enforces, so on real extractions demotion effectively never fires. Promotion to unique never happens in emitted DDL; safe new unique indexes are only suggested in the opportunities artifact (CREATE UNIQUE NONCLUSTERED INDEX text in safe-to-apply).

v2.behavior: Additive-only, opt-in: source-unique indexes ALWAYS emit UNIQUE (AlreadyUnique, 'trusted regardless of profile'); with a registered uniqueIndex intervention (both toggles default true when omitted), NON-unique indexes are PROMOTED to UNIQUE whenever the cache-derived candidate profile shows no duplicates (every attribute is a single-column candidate; every non-unique composite index is a composite candidate). Emitter applies decision as disjunction (isUnique || overlay.EnforceUnique) so a DoNotEnforce can never demote; promotion round-trips through the canary (M1 readback via sys.indexes.is_unique). Without a profile, NoCandidateProfiled withholds promotion.

findings.difference: Opposite polarities: V1 can only weaken declared uniqueness (never strengthen in DDL); V2 can only strengthen (never weaken). Under default configs on a physically-extracted estate both emit exactly the source-unique set (net parity). The behavioral trap is V2's promotion: registering the intervention with a live profile converts every currently-duplicate-free non-unique index (including kept platform-auto ones) into a UNIQUE index, making future duplicate inserts fail — a semantic change V1 confined to an advisory artifact. Conversely V1's demotion could silently loosen uniqueness on degraded models; V2 cannot.
findings.outsystemsRelevant: conditional — fires only when policy.tightening registers a uniqueIndex intervention with profile evidence attached; on default runs both pipelines emit the source-unique set for a physically-extracted estate
findings.empiricalCheck: Live lane: register {kind:'uniqueIndex', id:'u1'} with no booleans, attach live profile on localhost:11433 where a non-unique indexed column has no duplicates; diff DDL (index becomes CREATE UNIQUE INDEX [UIX_...]) and decision log EnforceUnique(SingleColumnNoDuplicates). Then insert a duplicate and re-run: index stays IX (DataHasDuplicates).

findings.topic: Name/uniqueness coherence when tightening changes uniqueness

v1.behavior: Index name prefix is computed from the MODEL's IsUnique while the emitted uniqueness comes from the tightening decision — a demoted unique index emits as CREATE INDEX [UIX_...] (UIX-prefixed but non-unique).
v1.cite: src/Osm.Smo/SmoIndexBuilder.cs:115-126 (name from index.IsUnique at 118, definition from enforceUnique at 131)

v2.behavior: Name prefix and emitted uniqueness use the same disjunction (isUnique || overlay.EnforceUnique), so IX_/UIX_ always agrees with the CREATE [UNIQUE] INDEX form by construction.

findings.difference: V1 can emit misleading UIX_-named non-unique indexes when its matrix demotes; V2 keeps name and constraint in lockstep (promotion renames IX->UIX too).
findings.outsystemsRelevant: conditional — needs a V1 demotion to fire (degraded/hand-authored model or missing physical evidence)
findings.empiricalCheck: Fixture lane: v1 model JSON with isUnique index, no onDisk kind, no profile, mode EvidenceGated -> observe CREATE INDEX [UIX_...] in v1 output.

findings.topic: Clustered/nonclustered capture (incl. post-#669 work)

v1.behavior: PK constraint always emits CLUSTERED (Clustered=true on both inline and table-level forms); no non-PK index can emit CLUSTERED: the extraction Kind is only PK/UQ/UIX/IX (type_desc never captured beyond HEAP-exclusion), IndexKind is dropped at SmoIndexBuilder.MapIndexMetadata, and IndexScriptBuilder never sets CreateIndexStatement.Clustered. IndexKind.ClusteredIndex exists but only hand-written JSON could produce it and emission ignores it.

v2.behavior: Identical posture: Index IR has no clustered field; single-column PK sets Clustered=true inline; the only other Clustered usage is the data-lane #temp staging index. WP-2 (clustering capture + heaps + emit per reality) is planned but NOT started as of this tree; no clustering-capture work landed in PRs #669/670/671.
v2.cite: sidecar/projection/src/Projection.Core/Catalog.fs:1066-1176 (no clustered field); sidecar/projection/src/Projection.Targets.SSDT/ScriptDomBuild.fs:713-715,2052-2058; SSDT_REMEDIATION_HANDOFF.md:32 ('Not started: ... WP-2'); sidecar/projection/SSDT_HANDOFF_REVIEW_PACKET.md D1

findings.difference: PARITY of the gap, refining the mined claim pkt-d1's 'v2-regression' framing: BOTH pipelines normalize a DBA-re-clustered non-PK index back to PK-clustered silently (packet D1 itself now says 'neither V1 nor V2 captures sys.indexes.type_desc'). Also shared: XML/spatial/columnstore index rows pass the HEAP-only filter and would emit as plain [UNIQUE] INDEX (invalid or wrong DDL) in both pipelines — no type_desc discrimination exists on either side.
findings.outsystemsRelevant: conditional — Service Studio cannot author clustered indexes; bites only DBA-re-clustered tables or DBA XML/spatial/columnstore indexes on estate tables
findings.empiricalCheck: Live lane: on localhost:11433 drop a PK's clustering and CREATE CLUSTERED INDEX on another column; run both extract+emit paths; both outputs show PRIMARY KEY CLUSTERED and a plain nonclustered secondary index.

findings.topic: Composite PK CLUSTERED/ASC keyword emission

v1.behavior: Table-level (composite) PK emits PRIMARY KEY CLUSTERED with explicit per-column ASC: 'CONSTRAINT [PK_Order_Id_Status] PRIMARY KEY CLUSTERED ([Id] ASC, [Status] ASC)' (Clustered=true set on the table-level constraint).

v2.behavior: Table-level composite PK omits CLUSTERED and ASC: primaryKeyConstraint never sets Clustered; golden pins 'CONSTRAINT [PK_Assignment_ProjectId_ResourceId] PRIMARY KEY ([ProjectId], [ResourceId])'. Single-column inline PK DOES state CLUSTERED (attachInlinePrimaryKey), matching V1.

findings.difference: Bytes differ on composite-PK tables (CLUSTERED + ASC keywords dropped by V2); semantics identical (PRIMARY KEY defaults to CLUSTERED when no other clustered index, ASC is default). Packet B1's 'composite PK omits CLUSTERED' is a V2 golden fact, not V1's rendered form — V1 emitted it.
findings.outsystemsRelevant: conditional — OutSystems entities are single-PK; composite PKs come from static join tables or DBA/external tables
findings.empiricalCheck: Fixture lane diff of any composite-PK table between v1 full-export output and v2 bundle.

findings.topic: Ordering of index statements and DISABLE placement

v1.behavior: Non-PK indexes emit grouped: unique first, then non-unique, insertion (model, i.e. alphabetical-by-source-name) order within group; a disabled index's ALTER INDEX ... DISABLE emits IMMEDIATELY after its own CREATE.

v2.behavior: Non-PK indexes emit sorted by SsKey (unique/non-unique interleaved per key order); ALL AlterIndexDisable statements emit after ALL CREATE INDEX statements, also SsKey-sorted.

findings.difference: Statement order within a table file differs (V1: UIX block then IX block, DISABLE interleaved; V2: SsKey order, DISABLEs batched last). Deploy semantics identical. Additionally the WITH-clause option order differs when >2 options present: V1 emits FILLFACTOR, PAD_INDEX, IGNORE_DUP_KEY, STATISTICS_NORECOMPUTE, ALLOW_ROW_LOCKS, ALLOW_PAGE_LOCKS, DATA_COMPRESSION; V2 emits FILLFACTOR, PAD_INDEX, ALLOW_ROW_LOCKS, ALLOW_PAGE_LOCKS, STATISTICS_NORECOMPUTE, IGNORE_DUP_KEY, DATA_COMPRESSION (src/Osm.Smo/PerTableEmission/IndexScriptBuilder.cs:193-257 vs sidecar/projection/src/Projection.Targets.SSDT/ScriptDomBuild.fs:2194-2233).
findings.outsystemsRelevant: yes for any diff-based review of v1-vs-v2 output; no runtime effect
findings.empiricalCheck: Fixture lane: multi-index table diff between the two outputs; count line positions of UIX vs IX and DISABLE statements.

findings.topic: Index options on V2's model-JSON source path

v1.behavior: The JSON deserializer consumes the FULL on-disk index envelope from osm_model.json: isDisabled, isPadded, fill_factor, ignoreDupKey, allowRowLocks, allowPageLocks, noRecompute, filterDefinition, dataSpace, partitionColumns, dataCompression, plus index extendedProperties — all reach emission.

v2.behavior: The model-JSON adapter reads only name/isPrimary/isUnique/columns(direction,isIncluded)/filterDefinition/isPlatformAuto; IsDisabled, FillFactor, PAD_INDEX, IGNORE_DUP_KEY, lock flags, STATISTICS_NORECOMPUTE, DataCompression, DataSpace and index ExtendedProperties stay at smart-constructor defaults. The in-code comment claims 'V1's JSON projection does not yet carry on-disk metadata' — false: #IdxJson carries all of it; only V2's reader skips it. The rowset/live path DOES carry these axes.

findings.difference: Same estate through the two V2 source lanes yields different index DDL: on the JSON lane a disabled index deploys ENABLED (its DISABLE statement is lost), IGNORE_DUP_KEY (insert-semantics!) and all storage options/filegroups/compression silently vanish, and index-level extended properties drop — everything V1 preserved on the identical input file. Silent loss with no tolerance token, violating the repo's named-erasure law.
findings.outsystemsRelevant: conditional — bites any operator feeding V2 from osm_model.json (the fixture/offline lane); the options themselves are DBA-authored (platform never sets them) but that is exactly the cutover surface
findings.empiricalCheck: Fixture lane: osm_model.json with an index carrying isDisabled:true + fill_factor:85 + ignoreDupKey:true; v1 full-export emits WITH (FILLFACTOR=85, IGNORE_DUP_KEY=ON) + ALTER INDEX DISABLE; v2 publish from the same JSON emits a bare CREATE INDEX.

findings.topic: DATA_COMPRESSION never parses on V2's live path (JSON key mismatch)

v1.behavior: Emits DATA_COMPRESSION per compression level with collapsed partition ranges (ON PARTITIONS (a TO b)), skipping NONE; levels include ROW/PAGE/COLUMNSTORE/COLUMNSTORE_ARCHIVE. Source JSON keys are [partition]/[compression].

v2.behavior: tryParseUniformDataCompression looks for a 'Code' property, but the byte-identical embedded SQL emits keys 'partition'/'compression' — so the parse always yields zero codes -> None, and Index.DataCompression is never populated from live extraction. The emitter path itself works (golden pins DATA_COMPRESSION = PAGE from a hand-built catalog) and, when given uniform NONE, would emit explicit DATA_COMPRESSION = NONE (no None-skip like V1's). No test feeds the parser real rowset JSON.
v2.cite: sidecar/projection/src/Projection.Adapters.OssysSql/MetadataSnapshotRunner.fs:961-980 (TryGetProperty("Code") at 971) vs sidecar/projection/src/Projection.Adapters.OssysSql/Resources/outsystems_metadata_rowsets.sql:552-553; sidecar/projection/src/Projection.Targets.SSDT/ScriptDomBuild.fs:2215-2233 (no NONE skip); golden Modules/Forms/dbo.ScalarGallery.sql:66

findings.difference: A compressed estate index (uniform PAGE/ROW) loses its DATA_COMPRESSION clause in V2 live-path output — deploying the ejected project silently DECOMPRESSES the index (storage-size and IO-profile change); V1 preserved it. Packet D3's 'uniform DATA_COMPRESSION emits' is true of the emitter but false end-to-end on the live path. Distinct residuals even after a fix: per-partition heterogeneous compression -> None (V1 emitted ranges), columnstore levels unsupported (adapter maps only NONE/ROW/PAGE), and V2 would emit explicit '= NONE' where V1 omitted it.
findings.outsystemsRelevant: conditional — compression is DBA-applied (platform never compresses); common on large on-prem OutSystems log/audit tables, i.e. the real cutover estate
findings.empiricalCheck: Live lane on localhost:11433: ALTER INDEX ... REBUILD WITH (DATA_COMPRESSION = PAGE) on a seeded table; run v2 publish from live source; emitted CREATE INDEX has no WITH clause. V1 full-export from same DB emits DATA_COMPRESSION = PAGE.

findings.topic: Round-trip verification of indexes (forward-only options)

v1.behavior: No deployed-reality readback verification leg exists for emitted indexes at all (emission fingerprint/coverage are input-side).
v1.cite: src/Osm.Pipeline/Orchestration/EmissionCoverageCalculator.cs:115 (input-side only); no readback module in src/Osm.*

v2.behavior: Canary readback compares index STRUCTURE — owner, emitted name, uniqueness (incl. EnforceUnique promotion, M1), ordered key columns with ASC/DESC — via ReadSide.readIndexes + PhysicalSchema.PhysicalIndex; INCLUDE columns, filter predicate and storage options are recovered by neither leg and are the named OpenGap tolerance IndexOptionsUnreflected. Emitted names are shared with the emitter via one IndexNaming derivation so emit->deploy->readback agrees by construction.

findings.difference: Refines mined claim pkt-d3: 'the verification leg recovers none of them' is stale — structure/uniqueness/DESC now round-trip (including promotion decisions); only options/INCLUDE/filter remain unverified (post-handoff drift in those axes is invisible to the tool, SSDT compare is the only guard). Net: V2 adds a verification capability V1 never had, with a named residual.
findings.outsystemsRelevant: yes — the canary is the cutover sign-off mechanism; its blind spots (options) are exactly the DBA-authored axes
findings.empiricalCheck: Docker lane: CanaryRoundTripTests M1 test; then mutate a deployed index's FILLFACTOR and confirm the canary still reports agreement (the documented blind spot).

findings.topic: Index referencing a column not in the model

v1.behavior: Silently drops the whole index when any column fails the attribute lookup (columnsBuilder cleared, loop continues) and silently drops included-only indexes — no diagnostic, index absent from output.

v2.behavior: Fails loud: resolveIndexColumnAttribute returns a typed adapter error ('indexColumnUnresolved ... no matching attribute in V2's IR'), aggregated up through parseKind/parseRowsetBundle — the whole run refuses. Post-Catalog.create, emitter-side resolution is a structural invariant (invalidOp unreachable).

findings.difference: V1 loses DBA indexes on unmodeled columns silently (schema drift invisible); V2 refuses the entire extraction until the operator handles it. Louder-but-stricter: an estate with one such index cannot publish at all in V2 without remediation.
findings.outsystemsRelevant: yes — DBA indexes on unmodeled (dropped-attribute or DBA-added) columns are common in aged estates; V2 turns silent loss into a blocking named error
findings.empiricalCheck: Live lane: add an index on a physical column with no OutSystems attribute; v1 full-export succeeds without the index, v2 publish fails with indexColumnUnresolved.

findings.topic: Generated index-name collisions (same key columns, same table)

v1.behavior: IndexNameGenerator derives purely from entity + key attributes + uniqueness; two distinct source indexes with identical key columns and uniqueness produce IDENTICAL generated names, and PerTableWriter has no dedupe — the per-table file contains two CREATE INDEX statements with the same name (deploy/build error). Masked by the default OSIDX prune.

v2.behavior: Proof-triggered disambiguation: colliding base names gain deterministic 1-based ordinals in SsKey order (IX_ScalarGallery_Code_1/_2/_3 pinned in golden); non-colliding names stay concise; all ride the identifier budget.

findings.difference: The OutSystems-shaped collision (platform OSIDX on an FK column + developer index on the same column) yields broken V1 output when platform-auto indexes are kept, valid disambiguated V2 output. Note the ordinal makes emitted names input-set-sensitive: pruning or adding a sibling index can renumber survivors across runs (rename-as-drop/create under schema compare).
findings.outsystemsRelevant: yes when keeping platform-auto indexes (V2's default); the duplicate-key-column index pattern is common
findings.empiricalCheck: Fixture lane: two non-unique indexes on the same column; v1 emits duplicate names (DacFx build error), v2 emits _1/_2. Then remove one and re-run v2: the survivor's name loses its ordinal (renumbering check).

findings.topic: Source UNIQUE constraints flatten to unique indexes (claim pkt-a7 refined)

v1.behavior: Extraction represents sys.key_constraints UQ as index rows (Kind='UQ', IsUnique=1); emission has no UNIQUE-constraint path (UniqueConstraintDefinition used only for PK), so UQ constraints emit as CREATE UNIQUE INDEX with a synthesized UIX_ name — constraint identity and name lost.

v2.behavior: Identical: the Kind='UQ' discriminator is dropped at the snapshot boundary (IndexRow has no Kind field), Uniqueness=ofLegacyBooleans(IsUnique,IsPrimary), emitter's only UniqueConstraintDefinition is the PK — UQ emits as CREATE UNIQUE INDEX [UIX_...].

findings.difference: Mined claim pkt-a7 frames this as a V2 divergence; it is v1<->v2 PARITY — an inherited shared deviation from the deployed source (object class changes UQ-constraint -> unique index on redeploy; schema compare on a live target shows drop-constraint+create-index in BOTH pipelines).
findings.outsystemsRelevant: conditional — the platform authors unique INDEXES, not UNIQUE constraints; UQ constraints arrive via DBA/external tables
findings.empiricalCheck: Live lane: ALTER TABLE ... ADD CONSTRAINT uq UNIQUE(col) on a seeded table; both pipelines emit CREATE UNIQUE INDEX (deploy and compare sys.key_constraints: empty both).

findings.topic: INCLUDE (covering) columns carriage (claim adm-uix-binary refresh)

v1.behavior: Carries included columns in IndexModel.Columns with IsIncluded and emits INCLUDE(...); tightening filters included columns out of key evaluation.

v2.behavior: Index.IncludedColumns landed (chapter 4.5 slice beta) and is read by BOTH adapters and emitted as INCLUDE(...); Index.Columns stays key-only so tightening evaluates keys by construction; golden pins INCLUDE([Amount]).

findings.difference: The mined ADMIRE claim 'included columns are dropped at the V2 boundary' is STALE — the drop was retired; both sides now emit INCLUDE and both exclude included columns from uniqueness evaluation and from generated names. Fixed on main well before this tree (chapter 4.5).
findings.outsystemsRelevant: conditional — INCLUDE columns are DBA-authored
findings.empiricalCheck: Fixture lane: covering index in model; both outputs contain INCLUDE([...]).

findings.topic: PK-less kinds (heaps): gate vs silence (claim pkt-d1 second half refined)

v1.behavior: Silent: with no primary index and no identifier attributes, the PK constraint is simply omitted (heap emitted), no diagnostic anywhere.

v2.behavior: Heap still emits (golden dbo.Heap.sql has no PK), but: (a) check-estate raises a heap advisory; (b) a Warning diagnostic structural.targetMissingPrimaryKey fires for missing-PK kinds that are FK TARGETS, with overrides.allowMissingPrimaryKey acting as an acceptance ANNOTATION (acceptedVia metadata), not an emission gate; (c) symmetric-closure inverse building skips such targets with a named reason.

findings.difference: Refines mined claim pkt-d1: 'must be allow-listed via overrides.allowMissingPrimaryKey' overstates — emission is not blocked either way; the allowlist only marks the warning accepted (and only referenced targets warn; an unreferenced heap gets the estate advisory only). Still a strict improvement over V1's total silence.
findings.outsystemsRelevant: conditional — OutSystems entities always have Id PKs; heaps arrive via external/DBA tables
findings.empiricalCheck: Fixture lane: PK-less kind referenced by another kind; v2 diagnostics contain structural.targetMissingPrimaryKey (with/without acceptedVia per allowlist); v1 output has no corresponding signal.

findings.topic: Index-level extended properties

v1.behavior: Only MS_Description is lifted from index extended properties (all other index-level extended properties dropped); the PK's description is addressed with @level2type=N'CONSTRAINT', non-PK with N'INDEX'.

v2.behavior: ALL Index.ExtendedProperties emit in source order, addressed to the EMITTED index name; owner is always @level2type=N'INDEX' — including for PK-marked indexes (whose emitted name is the PK constraint name). Golden pins an INDEX-level MS_Description.

findings.difference: V2 is a superset (arbitrary index extended properties survive; V1 dropped all but MS_Description). Divergence: a property on the PK's backing index lands on the INDEX object in V2 vs the CONSTRAINT object in V1 (different sys.extended_properties addressing; both deploy since PK index name = constraint name).
findings.outsystemsRelevant: conditional — index-level extended properties are DBA-authored metadata
findings.empiricalCheck: Fixture lane: index with a custom extended property; present in v2 output only. Deploy both and query sys.extended_properties class for the PK-description case.

findings.topic: Bare-table emission mode (indexes suppressed)

v1.behavior: emitTableMode BareOnly/Both produces per-table variants without indexes/extended properties/triggers (EmitBareTableOnly gate before index loop).

v2.behavior: No equivalent knob; EmissionPolicy gates schema/data/diagnostics families but has no bare-table variant — indexes always emit with the table when schema emits.

findings.difference: Minor operational capability V1 had (bare CREATE TABLE artifacts for staged bootstraps) that V2 does not expose. Default behavior (FullOnly) is identical.
findings.outsystemsRelevant: conditional — only if the operator's workflow consumed V1 bare variants
findings.empiricalCheck: Config sweep: no projection.json key reaches an index-suppressing per-table variant (CONFIG_REFERENCE.md has none).

findings.topic: categoricalUniqueness intervention is advisory-only (claim pkt-d5)

v1.behavior: No analog in DDL policy; nearest surface is the opportunities report / safe-to-apply.sql which SUGGESTS CREATE UNIQUE NONCLUSTERED INDEX statements for model-unique indexes but never alters emitted Modules/*.sql.

v2.behavior: CategoricalUniquenessPass produces a decision set; 'the catalog itself is unchanged — uniqueness suggestions are metadata'; consumers are fidelity/estate surfaces (ModelFidelity, Estate/EstateFinding UniquenessCandidates); no DDL emitter consumes it.

findings.difference: Claim confirmed: advisory-only, never touches DDL; a V2-growth capability with only a partial V1 analog (V1's suggestions came from declared-unique indexes; V2's from distribution inference).
findings.outsystemsRelevant: yes as a review aid; no output effect
findings.empiricalCheck: Fixture lane: register categoricalUniqueness; assert DDL byte-identical and fidelity report gains UniquenessCandidates.

findings.topic: Doc-vs-code drift on index rows (matrix + Policy comment + packet D2)

v1.behavior: n/a (documentation about V1 behavior): V1 default is prune (config/default-tightening.json:32) and V1's rowset lift landed in V2.

v2.behavior: V1_PARITY_MATRIX rows 15/16/26 still say indexes are 'not lifted; sourced via osm_model.json' and rows 55/56/122/183 still mark IgnoreDupKey/IsDisabled/DataCompression/DataSpace as 'deferred', but all landed (IndexRow fields + emit slices); Policy.fs doc-comment calls keep 'V1 default' (V1's was prune); packet D2 claims the OSIDX heuristic exists 'on the live path' (it does not — see idx-platform-auto-live-flag-dead).

findings.difference: Three V2 documentation surfaces contradict current code in the index area; per audit rules the contradictions are findings (the matrix understates V2's landed index fidelity; the Policy comment and packet D2 overstate V1-parity/live-path detection).
findings.outsystemsRelevant: yes — reviewers using the matrix/packet as decision registers will mis-scope index work
findings.empiricalCheck: Static: compare cited matrix rows against OssysRowsetTypes.fs/ScriptDomBuild.fs; grep sidecar F# for OSIDX (only the SQL resource matches).

findings.topic: Partition-scheme dataspace with unparseable partition columns

v1.behavior: Suppresses the ON clause when a PARTITION_SCHEME dataspace has no partition columns (defensive ShouldEmitDataSpaceClause), and suppresses PRIMARY-filegroup at emit time.

v2.behavior: PRIMARY suppression moved to the snapshot boundary (reflection can no longer produce ON [PRIMARY]; an authored one still renders — pinned by test); but a PARTITION_SCHEME whose PartitionColumnsJson fails to parse degrades to Some(PartitionScheme(name, [])) and the emitter renders ON [scheme] with no columns (no V1-style empty-columns suppression).

findings.difference: Malformed/absent partition-column JSON yields invalid DDL (ON [scheme] without a column) in V2 where V1 suppressed the clause. Reachable only on malformed reflection JSON — the SQL always emits >=1 partition_ordinal>0 column for a real scheme. Core dataspace carriage (dec-dataspace claim) otherwise confirmed at parity.
