The differing default lives at TighteningOptions.cs:62 (false) vs Policy.fs:758/Config.fs:531 (true).

findings.empiricalFact: Customer.Email is COLLATE Latin1_General_CI_AI in ParityEstate (sqlcmd-verified); V1's DDL emits no COLLATE (v1-live dbo.Customer.sql:9) so the deployed column fell to the database default SQL_Latin1_General_CP1_CI_AS (catalog-v1.txt:21); V2 emits the COLLATE clause and matches source (catalog-v2.txt:21).
findings.mechanism: V1 does NOT lose collation at extraction (c.collation_name captured, outsystems_model_export.sql:352) nor in the SMO model (SmoColumnBuilder.cs:41 carries onDisk.Collation). The drop is a hardcoded suppression at render: BuildColumnDefinition emits definition.Collation only when the value is non-empty AND not equal to 'Latin1_General_CI_AI' (case-insensitive) — CreateTableStatementBuilder.cs:313-317. The OutSystems platform collation is treated as 'the default, omit it'; on any target DB whose default differs, the omitted clause silently rebinds the column to the target default. V2: the rowset reader carries sys.columns.collation_name per column (OssysRowsetReader.fs:74-81, audit-F1 comment 'carry the source-declared collation so the emit re-states COLLATE') and ScriptDomBuild emits COLLATE whenever the IR has Some collation (ScriptDomBuild.fs:454-464).
findings.fixCost: Trivial: delete the equality special-case (or compare against the TARGET database default instead of a hardcoded platform collation). WP coverage: none — packet C8 is an open [HARD/GAP] register row (V2's residual gap is the inverse: default-collation columns inherit the target default; the fix there is pinning the target DB collation as a deploy prerequisite).
findings.notes: The suppressed value is exactly the collation the whole OutSystems estate uses, so the bug is invisible whenever the target DB is created with the platform collation — the ParityV1DB used the SQL Server default and exposed it.

findings.empiricalFact: SyncLog.RawXml deploys as nvarchar(max) from V1 and as xml from V2 (catalog.diff:57); the ParityEstate source column OSUSR_INT_SYNCLOG.RAWXML is XML (sqlcmd-verified; seed ossys-edge-case.seed.sql:463) — V2 matches the deployed source, V1 does not.
findings.mechanism: V1's model and type-resolution are actually CORRECT: the extracted attribute has dataType 'xml' and onDisk.sqlType 'xml'; TypeMappingPolicy.Resolve prefers the on-disk rule (TypeMappingPolicy.cs:41-45), the embedded config maps onDisk 'xml' -> 'xml' (config/type-mapping.default.json:154, embedded via Osm.Smo.csproj:17), and the rule constructs SMO DataType(SqlDataType.Xml) (TypeMappingRule.cs:55). The loss is the ScriptDom TRANSLATOR: CreateTableStatementBuilder.MapSqlDataType has no case for SqlDataType.Xml — the catch-all `_ => SqlDataTypeOption.NVarChar` (CreateTableStatementBuilder.cs:541) rewrites it, and TranslateDataType appends MAX because MaximumLength<=0 (:484-489) -> NVARCHAR(MAX). V2: OssysTypeMapping.tryParse maps raw 'xml' -> (Text, SqlStorageType.Xml) (OssysTypeMapping.fs:96) via resolveAttributeType (OssysTranslation.fs:362-369), and the DDL renders the storage type faithfully.
findings.cite: v1: src/Osm.Smo/PerTableEmission/CreateTableStatementBuilder.cs:513-542 (catch-all at :541) + :484-489; correct upstream: src/Osm.Smo/TypeMappingPolicy.cs:41-45 + src/Osm.Smo/TypeMappingRule.cs:55. v2: sidecar/projection/src/Projection.Core/OssysTypeMapping.fs:96 + sidecar/projection/src/Projection.Adapters.Osm/OssysTranslation.fs:362-369
findings.fixCost: Trivial: add SqlDataType.Xml (and the other missing legacy cases — Text/NText/Image also route through the same catch-all) to MapSqlDataType. WP coverage: none V1-side; on V2 the area is governed by C1/WP-4b (LANDED — deployed-storage precedence) and WP-17(c) (xml data-plane carriage, LANDED), both already correct here.
findings.notes: A silent-catch-all bug class: every SqlDataType the switch does not enumerate becomes NVARCHAR(MAX) with no diagnostic. V1's own type-mapping config advertises xml support, making the emitted nvarchar look like a mapping decision when it is a renderer hole.

findings.empiricalFact: Source FK_OSUSR_INV_MOVEMENT_SUPPLIER is enabled-but-untrusted (is_not_trusted=1, is_disabled=0; sqlcmd-verified). V1's deployed copy is DISABLED (catalog-v1: FK_StockMovement_Supplier_SupplierId 1 1) via WITH NOCHECK ADD followed by NOCHECK CONSTRAINT (v1-live StockMovement.sql:14-19); V2's copy is enabled untrusted (1 0) via NOCHECK CONSTRAINT then WITH NOCHECK CHECK CONSTRAINT (v2-live StockMovement.sql:20-24).
findings.mechanism: V1: BuildNoCheckForeignKeyStatements emits 'ALTER TABLE ... WITH NOCHECK ADD CONSTRAINT ...' (CreateTableStatementBuilder.cs:273-276) — which alone already yields enabled+untrusted — then, when foreignKey.IsNoCheck, appends 'ALTER TABLE ... NOCHECK CONSTRAINT ...' (:279-282). That second statement DISABLES the constraint (is_disabled=1). The in-code comment at :278 — 'If the FK should remain unchecked (untrusted), emit NOCHECK CONSTRAINT to disable it' — shows the disabling is intended-as-written but rests on conflating untrusted with disabled; the produced state diverges from the source's enabled-untrusted. IsNoCheck here is the REFLECTED deployed trust state (ForeignKeyEvidenceResolver.cs:129-130 reality.IsNoCheck), independent of the allowNoCheckCreation toggle (SmoForeignKeyBuilder.cs:24,39; config default false). V2: untrustedFkAltersUsing emits the two-step pair [AlterTableDisableConstraint; AlterTableNoCheckConstraint] when not fk.IsConstraintTrusted (SsdtDdlEmitter.fs:418-432); buildAlterTableDisableConstraint renders 'NOCHECK CONSTRAINT' (ScriptDomBuild.fs:2386-2395 — its doc names the intermediate is_disabled=1 state as leg 1) and buildAlterTableNoCheckConstraint renders 'WITH NOCHECK CHECK CONSTRAINT' (ScriptDomBuild.fs:2367-2377 — re-enable going forward, skip validating existing rows) -> final state enabled untrusted, matching sys.foreign_keys reality.
findings.fixCost: Trivial: replace V1's second statement with WITH NOCHECK CHECK CONSTRAINT (V2's leg 2), or drop it entirely (WITH NOCHECK ADD already leaves the FK untrusted). WP coverage: packet B6 [HARD, verdict open] documents V2's two-step as 'order verified against SQL Server' and faithful; WP-9 extends the same trust-state reproduction to CHECK constraints; no V1-side WP.
findings.notes: Answer to 'is disabling intended?': the emission of the disable statement is deliberate (guarded by IsNoCheck, with an explanatory comment), but the comment's own wording shows the author believed NOCHECK CONSTRAINT = 'keep untrusted' — the disabled end-state is an unintended semantic, not an unintended statement. Consequence beyond trust: a disabled FK enforces nothing at all, so V1's deployed DB silently accepts orphan SupplierIds.

findings.topic: manifest.json schema & consumer contract
findings.v1: PascalCase SsdtManifest: Tables[] with Indexes/ForeignKeys NAME lists + IncludesExtendedProperties, Options block (IncludePlatformAutoIndexes etc.), PolicySummary (counts, rationale histograms, ModuleRollups, TogglePrecedence), Emission{SHA256 hash}, Coverage, PredicateCoverage, PreRemediation, Unsupported
findings.v2: camelCase manifest: emitter/version header, registry.digest + policy{digest,version}, tables[] with counts only, coverage, predicateCoverage, unsupported (12 static tolerances), plus deploymentBatches, appliedTransforms (78), centrality, boundedContexts, schemaComplexity, profileAnomalies, queryHints, policyConflicts, columnProfiles
findings.difference: Despite v2's stated intent to mirror V1's SsdtManifest shape, casing, per-table fields, and section sets diverge; only tableFile relative paths (Modules/<Module>/<schema>.<table>.sql) and the coverage skeleton are drop-in compatible. A v1-manifest consumer cannot parse v2's manifest without adaptation, and vice versa.

findings.topic: v2 per-table foreignKeyCount vs emitted DDL
findings.v1: Lists FK constraint names on the owning table only: 8 total, exactly matching 8 inline FOREIGN KEY clauses in emitted DDL (Customer 1, StockMovement 2, JobRun 1, Category 1, OrderLine 1, SalesOrder 2)
findings.v2: foreignKeyCount = List.length k.References post-composition, which includes symmetric-closure inverse edges: sums to 16; City/User/StockItem/Supplier report 1 with 0 FKs in their DDL, Customer 2 vs 1 emitted, Category 3 vs 1, SalesOrder 3 vs 2
findings.difference: v2's field doc (ManifestEmitter.fs:523-525 'Count of FK constraints emitted (inline) for this table') is contradicted by the emitted values on 6 of 15 tables because the SymmetricClosure pass doubles every reference edge; consumers reconciling manifest against bundle DDL get false counts. v1's name lists verify exactly.

findings.topic: coverage.constraints denominator
findings.v1: Constraints 28/28 (100%)
findings.v2: Constraints 35/35 (100%) = 15 PKs + 3 non-PK unique + 16 references + 1 check; the 16 references double-count the 8 physical FK edges via symmetric closure (true total would be 27)
findings.difference: Both claim 100% but the constraint universes are incomparable, and v2's is inflated by the same doubled reference list as the per-table counts. Tables 15/15 and columns 55/55 agree exactly on both sides.

findings.topic: coverage tables/columns axes
findings.v1: Tables 15/15, Columns 55/55, 100%
findings.v2: tables 15/15, columns 55/55, 100%
findings.difference: Exact agreement on table and column coverage over the same estate (56th source column is the inactive/computed one both sides exclude from emission).

findings.topic: manifest.summary.txt vs v1 tightening reporting in default flows
findings.v1: Default full-export always runs EvidenceGated tightening and reports it: 41/56 columns tightened, 18/18 unique indexes enforced, 8/8 FKs created, with rationale histograms in manifest/policy files and full console narration
findings.v2: manifest.summary.txt renders 0 decision(s) in all six buckets and 'Totals: 0 structural tightening(s)' — Pipeline.fs decisionSetsOf returns empty sets because the default publish flow registers no tightening interventions; NOT NULL in DDL comes from the logical model, not decisions
findings.difference: The one operator artifact v2 ships to answer 'how much did tightening do this run' is all-zeros on the default flow for the same estate where v1 reports 41 tightenings. Technically true for v2's configuration, but the parity artifact is empty exactly where v1's is informative, and no per-column decision record exists in the v2 bundle.

findings.topic: v1 PredicateCoverage FK-constraint predicates
findings.v1: Flags HasLogicalForeignKeyWithoutDbConstraint on ALL 6 logical-FK tables and never emits HasLogicalForeignKeyWithDbConstraint; ground truth in its own extracted model: 7 of 8 references have hasDbConstraint=1 (only JobRun.TriggeredByUserId lacks one)
findings.v2: Distinguishes both predicates: HasLogicalForeignKeyWithDbConstraint on 8 tables, Without on JobRun+User only — matching reality (User appears via the symmetric-closure inbound edge)
findings.difference: v1's importer binds JsonPropertyName "reference_hasDbConstraint" (ModelJsonDeserializer.AttributeDocument.cs:75) but v1's own live extractor writes "hasDbConstraint", so the flag deserializes null→false and every reference looks unconstrained to the coverage calculator (EmissionCoverageCalculator.cs:377). FK decisions recover via profiler fkReality (DB_CONSTRAINT_PRESENT×7) so DDL is right, but the manifest metadata misleads operators about which tables need constraint remediation.

findings.topic: predicate semantics under identical names
findings.v1: HasUniqueIndex=15 (PKs counted as unique indexes), HasCompositeUniqueIndex=2 (includes OrderLine's composite PK), HasInactiveColumns=1, keys are PHYSICAL table names (OSUSR_ABC_CUSTOMER); 12 predicate names
findings.v2: HasUniqueIndex=3 (non-PK only), HasCompositeUniqueIndex=1, HasInactiveColumns=0 (evaluated on post-projection catalog where the inactive column is already erased), keys are LOGICAL names (Customer); 16 predicate names incl. HasTemporalHistory/IsInactiveEntity/HasExtendedProperties
findings.difference: Same field name, incomparable numbers: v1 evaluates the source model with PK-inclusive unique semantics under physical names; v2 evaluates the projected catalog with PK-exclusive semantics under logical names. Any cross-version predicate comparison or trend dashboard silently breaks.

findings.topic: extended-properties reporting accuracy
findings.v1: IncludesExtendedProperties=true on exactly the 8 tables whose emitted files contain sp_addextendedproperty (verified 1:1 against DDL)
findings.v2: predicateCounts HasExtendedProperties=0, yet all 15 v2 table files emit extended-property statements (4-20 occurrences each); the predicate reads the catalog's empty extendedProperties[] arrays while descriptions ride a separate field that IS emitted
findings.difference: v2's manifest denies a feature its own bundle exhibits on every table; v1's per-table flag is exact. Consumers auditing metadata emission from the manifest get a false negative on v2.

findings.topic: hashes recorded in manifests
findings.v1: Emission {Algorithm: SHA256, Hash} — canonical fingerprint over options + full table model + decision sets (EmissionFingerprintCalculator.cs); verified byte-stable across the determinism re-run
findings.v2: registry.digest (SHA256 of the transform-registry metadata) + policy{digest, version} — input-side stamps; no fingerprint of the emitted output; the substitute is whole-dist byte-determinism plus diffable catalog.snapshot.json
findings.difference: v1 lets a consumer detect output drift with one string compare; v2 lets a consumer detect it by diffing the (byte-deterministic) tree or snapshot but records no single output digest. v2's digests answer the complementary question (did the transform set / policy change) that v1 answers only via TogglePrecedence values.

findings.topic: operator questions only v1's reports answer (default flows)
findings.v1: policy-decisions.json / policy-decision-report.json: per-item decisions (56 columns, 18 indexes, 8 FKs) each with rationale codes, MakeNotNull/RequiresRemediation/CreateConstraint/ScriptWithNoCheck flags, module rollups, and 16 toggles with value + source; validations.json: 65 per-constraint dossiers with probe evidence (outcome, sample size, captured-at, orphan counts, constraint trust); profiles/manifest.json: snapshot counts + tightening insights
findings.v2: No per-column/index/FK decision or evidence artifact in the default publish bundle; suggest-config.json empty; policy represented only as digest+version
findings.difference: "Why is this column NOT NULL / why was this FK created / what evidence backs each constraint / which toggle values were in effect and from where" are answerable from v1's bundle and unanswerable from v2's default bundle.

findings.topic: operator questions only v2's reports answer
findings.v1: No data-fidelity report (validation types limited to Nullability/UniqueIndex/ForeignKey; no length/type-overflow category), no tolerance ledger, no value distributions (profile.json is aggregate counts), no post-projection model snapshot, no deployment-order record in the manifest, no environment/version history
findings.v2: fidelity.json/txt: 4 violation categories incl. Length/type overflow + acceptedDivergences (fired tolerances) + uniqueness candidates; distributions.json: per-column value frequencies with probe outcomes; catalog.snapshot.json: full round-trippable projected model; manifest deploymentBatches (topological deploy order), appliedTransforms ledger (78 entries with overlay classification), centrality/boundedContexts/schemaComplexity/profileAnomalies/queryHints; lifecycle/onprem.json: per-environment episode ledger (version ordinal/label, timestamp, full schema, cdcCaptureCount, applied transforms)
findings.difference: "Is the data consistent with the declared model, which tolerances fired, what shipped to DEV at v0, in what order do tables deploy, what transforms touched the model, which entities are structurally central" are answerable only from v2 artifacts.

findings.topic: 'unsupported' manifest field semantics
findings.v1: Unsupported: [] — per-run list of actually-unsupported features encountered (empty on this estate)
findings.v2: unsupported: 12 entries (BooleanCanonicalizationTolerated … TriggerBodyUnparsedDropped) — the STATIC closed catalogue of tolerated V1↔V2 divergences, emitted identically on every run (Unsupported.compute() takes unit); the fired subset lives in fidelity.acceptedDivergences, which is [] this run ('no tolerance fired this run')
findings.difference: Same field name, opposite population semantics: a consumer reading v2's manifest as per-run findings sees 12 permanent 'problems'; one reading v1's sees zero and misses that v2-class erasures exist at all. Cross-version dashboards on this field are meaningless.

findings.topic: remediation verdict on the live estate
findings.v1: needs-remediation.sql + safe-to-apply.sql: 'Total Opportunities: 0'; opportunities.json TotalCount 0; RemediationColumnCount 0
findings.v2: manifest.remediation.sql: 'No remediation candidates surfaced'; fidelity.json dataViolations total 0 across all 4 categories
findings.difference: Independent engines reached the identical verdict — the estate's data is fully consistent with the declared model and nothing needs remediation. Cross-validates both analysis paths.

findings.topic: remediation script contract
findings.v1: Two scripts split by machine-judged disposition (safe-to-apply vs needs-remediation) with 'Generated: <UTC>' headers and 'will not auto-execute' disclaimer; backed by opportunities.json taxonomy (Disposition/Category/Type/Risk); when non-empty, v1 generates sentinel-driven backfill pre-scripts (remediation.generatePreScripts, sentinels text/date/numeric, maxRowsDefaultBackfill 100000)
findings.v2: Single manifest.remediation.sql with per-finding three-option contract — OPTION 1 SELECT active, OPTION 2 UPDATE and OPTION 3 DELETE commented out with <DEFAULT>/<VALID_TARGET_KEY>/<CanonicalKey> placeholders (RemediationEmitter.fs); deduped against fidelity data violations; no timestamp (deterministic)
findings.difference: v1 pre-classifies actionability and can auto-generate executable backfills; v2 keeps the operator in the loop with only inspection active and destructive options requiring explicit uncommenting. Different risk philosophies; both explicit about non-execution.

findings.topic: pipeline-telemetry.zip vs bench/NDJSON/episode ledger
findings.v1: pipeline-telemetry.zip contains NO telemetry — it re-zips 4 already-emitted artifacts (manifest.json, policy-decisions.json, suggestions/*.sql; verified byte-equal to the out/ copies). Only timing data anywhere: 4 stage entries in full-export.manifest.json (extract-model has StartedAtUtc/Duration null), plus 4x-duplicated schema-validation warnings
findings.v2: bench/full-export/<ULID>.json: 231 labeled timers with Count/Total/Min/Max/Mean/P50/P95/P99 (stage.pipeline 2863ms, stage.extract 2337ms, per-emitter/per-adapter labels); run.ndjson: ~107KB structured event stream, 20 event codes (config.runStart, stage progress with elapsedMs, transform.diagnostic, structural.cycleUnresolved x6, centrality/boundedContext/schemaComplexity computed, summary.runComplete aggregates); notices/model-read/<ULID>.json: 44 typed divergence notices (28 storage, 15 primary-key, 1 nullability, with per-column messages); lifecycle/onprem.json episode ledger
findings.difference: v2's operational observability is categorically deeper: percentile-grade hot-path timing, a replayable structured event log with named diagnostic codes, and typed adapter notices; v1 offers three stage durations and a redundantly-packaged zip whose name promises telemetry it does not contain.

findings.topic: v1 rerun byte-instability: exact per-file variance
findings.v1: 11 of 28 out/ artifacts differ across identical back-to-back runs (1965 diff lines): [1] Bootstrap/AllEntitiesIncludingStatic.bootstrap.sql and [2] PostDeployment-Bootstrap.sql — '-- Generated: <ts> UTC' header line only; [3] suggestions/needs-remediation.sql and [4] safe-to-apply.sql — same header timestamp only; [5] opportunities.json — GeneratedAtUtc only; [6] validations.json — GeneratedAtUtc + ~308 CapturedAtUtc/'Captured=' probe-timestamp lines; [7] full-export.manifest.json — GeneratedAtUtc, per-stage Started/Completed/Duration, absolute artifact paths, AND hash-order churn of the Artifacts dictionaries; [8] manifest.json, [9] policy-decisions.json, [10] policy-decision-report.json — PURE dictionary iteration-order churn (ColumnRationales/UniqueIndexRationales/ForeignKeyRationales at top and per-module, ModuleRollups module order, TogglePrecedence toggle order) with identical values; [11] pipeline-telemetry.zip — binary (entry mtimes + repackaged unstable files). NO GUID/ULID variance, NO semantic value drift (all 6 JSONs byte-identical after key-sort + timestamp/path normalization), Emission.Hash stable, and the Modules/*.sql, Seeds, sqlproj DDL payload is byte-identical
findings.v2: diff -r of dist/ across reruns: 0 lines — the entire deliverable tree byte-identical including manifest.json, catalog.snapshot.json, distributions.json (probe capturedAtUtc zeroed to 0001-01-01), fidelity, remediation, summary
findings.difference: v1's nondeterminism is exactly three mechanical classes — embedded wall clocks, .NET hash-order dictionary serialization, embedded absolute paths — never content; v2 engineered all three out of its deliverables (T1 byte-determinism holds empirically). v1's reports cannot be diffed or content-addressed across runs without normalization tooling.

findings.topic: separation of deliverables from run-varying records
findings.v1: Mixes clocked reports and the telemetry zip into the same out/ directory as the DDL bundle: 11 of 28 files unstable, so no directory-level byte contract exists; artifacts also embed machine-absolute paths (full-export.manifest.json, profiles/manifest.json), making them non-portable
findings.v2: dist/ carries only deterministic deliverables; every run-varying record (run.ndjson, bench/, notices/ under ULID filenames, lifecycle episode 'at' timestamp) lives outside dist/; episode schema payload verified identical across reruns with only the coordinate timestamp differing
findings.difference: v2's layout gives CI a one-line parity check (diff -r) and content-addressable bundles; the cost is that in-bundle generation provenance (when/by-which-run) moves to the episode ledger and run stream instead of the artifacts themselves.

findings.topic: run-level manifest / stage ledger
findings.v1: full-export.manifest.json: machine-readable run record (config path, per-stage start/complete/duration/warnings, dynamic-artifact inventory with content types, static-seed listing) and a downstream consumer: the verify-data verb defaults --manifest to full-export.manifest.json; weaknesses: first stage untimed, the same 5-line schema warning repeated 4x, absolute paths throughout
findings.v2: No run manifest in dist/; the run record is split across stderr run.ndjson (structured, per-stage events), stdout artifact listing (28 files with sizes), and the episode ledger; nothing in the bundle names the artifacts it contains beyond manifest.json's table files
findings.difference: v1 ships a self-describing run contract another verb consumes; v2's equivalent is stream-based and requires capturing stderr/stdout — richer data, weaker at-rest contract for tooling that starts from the output directory alone.

findings.topic: recording of effective options/config in artifacts
findings.v1: manifest.json Options {IncludePlatformAutoIndexes, EmitBareTableOnly, SanitizeModuleNames, ModuleParallelism} + TogglePrecedence: 16 tightening toggles each with resolved Value and Source — a complete audit of what configuration produced the output
findings.v2: policy {digest, version} only; emission options (sqlproj/dacpac requested, constraint rendering, folders) are not recorded anywhere in dist/ — verified: the v2-live-proj manifest is byte-identical to v2-live's despite different emission options and a failed dacpac
findings.difference: From a v2 bundle alone an operator cannot reconstruct the run's effective options nor see that this bundle was produced by a run whose dacpac lane failed (EXIT=2, emitter.dacpac.failed SQL71001 visible only in run.ndjson); v1 embeds resolved options and toggle provenance in the deliverable.

findings.topic: raw data values in report artifacts
findings.v1: profiles/profile.json carries aggregates only (row/null counts, duplicate booleans, orphan counts; NullSample/OrphanSample null on this estate)
findings.v2: dist/onprem/distributions.json embeds actual cell values with frequencies (e.g. 'alice@example.com', 'Alice', 'Smith' for Customer columns; 4 categorical distributions on this estate)
findings.difference: v2's deliverable bundle contains raw source data values, which answers 'what values dominate this column' but places potentially sensitive data in an artifact that travels with the schema bundle; v1's reports never leave aggregate space in this run.

findings.topic: per-table index entries reflect an emission difference
findings.v1: manifest Indexes[] names 5 non-PK indexes; JobRun lists none and its DDL contains no CREATE INDEX (IncludePlatformAutoIndexes=false filters it)
findings.v2: manifest indexCount sums 6; JobRun reports 1 and its DDL emits CREATE INDEX [IX_JobRun_CreatedOn]
findings.difference: Each manifest is internally consistent with its own DDL on index counts (unlike FK counts), but they disagree with each other because v2 emits an index v1's platform-auto filter drops — a manifest-visible symptom of a DDL-emission policy difference (v1's filter toggle is recorded in Options; v2 records no equivalent).

findings.topic: artifact naming
findings.v1: Output model artifact named model.extracted.json; config referenced by path in run manifest
findings.v2: dist/onprem/projection.json is the emitted logical-model JSON, sharing its filename with the CLI's input config file projection.json in the working directory
findings.difference: Two unrelated files named projection.json in one workflow (input config at run root, model emit inside dist/) invites operator confusion when zipping/searching bundles.

findings.topic: persisted human-readable reporting
findings.v1: Rich human narration (per-module rollups, tightening prose, toggle table) exists only on console stdout — not persisted as an artifact unless the operator redirects it; persisted reports are all JSON/SQL
findings.v2: Persists two deterministic prose artifacts in the bundle: manifest.summary.txt (six-bucket tightening rollup, empty this run) and fidelity.txt (masthead + violations/divergences/candidates verdict in operator register)
findings.difference: v2 treats operator prose as a first-class deliverable; v1 treats it as ephemeral console output. (Content-wise the v2 summary is empty in the default flow — see v2-summary-zero-decisions.)

findings.topic: Nullability tightening divergence (Category.ParentCategoryId, StockMovement.SupplierId)
findings.v1: [ParentCategoryId] BIGINT NOT NULL; [SupplierId] BIGINT NOT NULL — v1's tightening policy promoted estate-NULL columns to NOT NULL based on profiling (profiles/profile.json: IsNullablePhysical:true, RowCount:0, NullCount:0 for both)
findings.v2: [ParentCategoryId] BIGINT NULL; [SupplierId] BIGINT NULL — preserves estate physical nullability (seed: SUPPLIERID INT NULL, ParentCategoryId nullable)
findings.difference: Two columns differ in NULL/NOT NULL between the emitted DDL and the deployed databases (roundtrip catalog.diff confirms is_nullable 0 vs 1 for both). v1's NOT NULL also forces it to drop the SET NULL delete rule on the Supplier FK (the FK-action delta itself is excluded). On v1's schema, inserting a root category (NULL parent) or a movement without supplier fails; tightening fired on zero-row evidence.

findings.topic: XML column type mapping (SyncLog.RawXml)
findings.v1: [RawXml] NVARCHAR (MAX) NULL — emits the OutSystems logical type (Text) from the model
findings.v2: [RawXml] XML NULL — emits the physical catalog type (seed: [RAWXML] XML NULL)
findings.difference: Deployed type differs (catalog.diff: nvarchar(-1) vs xml). v1 loses XML typing — XML validation, .value()/.query() methods, and XML storage format are absent in the v1-deployed schema; v2 matches the estate exactly.

findings.topic: Computed-column expression identifier remapping (Product.DisplayLabel)
findings.v1: AS (concat([Sku], N'-', CONVERT (NVARCHAR (20), [Price]))) — identifiers inside the expression remapped to projected logical column names
findings.v2: AS (concat([SKU], N'-', CONVERT (NVARCHAR (20), [PRICE]))) — raw catalog expression text with physical column casing preserved
findings.difference: v2 does not rewrite column references inside computed expressions to the projected column names; verified persisted into deployed catalogs (ParityV1DB definition uses [Sku]/[Price], ParityV2DB uses [SKU]/[PRICE]). Binds only because the DB collation is case-insensitive; under a case-sensitive catalog collation, or wherever a physical name differs from the logical name by more than case, v2's script would fail to bind.

findings.topic: GO placement after CREATE TRIGGER (v1) — ALTER folded into trigger body
findings.v1: No GO between CREATE TRIGGER and the following ALTER TABLE ... DISABLE TRIGGER (JobRun, StockMovement); verified in ParityV1DB sys.sql_modules that TR_JobRun_AUDIT and TR_StockMovement_UPD bodies CONTAIN the ALTER statement, both triggers remain is_disabled=0 (estate had them disabled), and the embedded ALTER references the stale PHYSICAL trigger name (TR_OSUSR_XYZ_JOBRUN_AUDIT / TR_OSUSR_INV_MOVEMENT_UPD) which does not exist post-rename, so any INSERT on JobRun or UPDATE on StockMovement in the v1 DB raises a runtime error
findings.v2: GO emitted between every batch (comment / CREATE TRIGGER / ALTER each separated); its trigger deltas (physical table/name targets failing to create) are the excluded trigger class
findings.difference: Distinct mechanical batching defect in v1 within the otherwise-excluded trigger surface: missing batch separator silently corrupts the deployed trigger instead of restoring the disabled state. Reported because 'GO placement' is a separate class from the known emission-shape/naming deltas; deploy log shows no error (statement became trigger body, so nothing failed at deploy time).

findings.topic: Time-type precision facet spelling (JobRun.CreatedOn, SalesOrder.PlacedAt, StockItem.LastSyncedAt/OpenTime, StockMovement.MovedAt)
findings.v1: DATETIME2, DATETIMEOFFSET, TIME — default scale omitted
findings.v2: DATETIME2 (7), DATETIMEOFFSET (7), TIME (7) — explicit default scale (SSDT-style, matches estate DDL text)
findings.difference: Semantically identical (7 is the default scale; deployed catalogs agree). Side effect: the type column in the aligned CREATE TABLE grid widens, shifting NULL/NOT NULL alignment on every row of the affected tables — the alignment shift is derivative of this class, not independent. All other facet spellings (NVARCHAR (n), DECIMAL (p, s), VARBINARY (MAX), VARCHAR (n)) are byte-identical.

findings.topic: CHECK constraint predicate parenthesization (dbo.Product only)
findings.v1: CONSTRAINT [CK_OSUSR_SAL_PRODUCT_PRICE] CHECK ([Price] >= (0))
findings.v2: CONSTRAINT [CK_OSUSR_SAL_PRODUCT_PRICE] CHECK (([Price] >= (0))) — one extra wrapping paren layer
findings.difference: v2 wraps the catalog definition (which already carries outer parens) in the CHECK's own parens. SQL Server normalizes on create: both deployed DBs store ([Price]>=(0)) (roundtrip catalog line 106 identical). Product is the only table with a CHECK constraint; billing.BillingAccount has none in either output.

findings.topic: Platform-auto index presence (IX_JobRun_CreatedOn) — clarification of a known exclusion
findings.v1: No index emitted anywhere for JobRun, although v1's model.extracted.json contains OSIDX_JOBRUN_CREATEDON (isPlatformAuto:1, DESC, allowRowLocks:false); omission is deliberate default policy (TighteningOptions.cs:62 includePlatformAutoIndexes:false)
findings.v2: CREATE INDEX [IX_JobRun_CreatedOn] ON [dbo].[JobRun]([CreatedOn] DESC) WITH (ALLOW_ROW_LOCKS = OFF) — renamed from OSIDX, preserves DESC key and non-default ALLOW_ROW_LOCKS, suppresses default ALLOW_PAGE_LOCKS = ON and NONCLUSTERED keyword
findings.difference: The excluded list characterizes this as one of three 'FK/index name deltas', but it is a presence delta: v1 filters OSIDX_* platform-auto indexes entirely (configurable), v2 emits them. Deployed: index exists only in ParityV2DB (catalog.diff 81a82). Also the only DESC-key and ALLOW_ROW_LOCKS instance in the estate, so those emission behaviors are v2-only evidence.

findings.topic: PERSISTED dropped from computed column by BOTH (Product.DisplayLabel)
findings.v1: AS (concat(...)) — no PERSISTED keyword
findings.v2: AS (concat(...)) — no PERSISTED keyword
findings.difference: Estate source is [DISPLAYLABEL] AS (...) PERSISTED (seed line 364); both emitters drop it and both deployed DBs show is_persisted=0. Identical behavior (v1-v2 parity) but a shared fidelity gap versus the estate — flagged so it is not mistaken for full computed-column parity.

findings.topic: Statement terminators and EOF whitespace
findings.v1: Semicolon-terminates extended-property EXECs (excluded class), ALTER TABLE ... NOCHECK CONSTRAINT, and ALTER TABLE ... DISABLE TRIGGER lines, but not other statements; files end with a single trailing newline
findings.v2: Never emits semicolons; every file ends with a trailing blank line (statement + \n\n, verified by od on 6 files)
findings.difference: Uniform terminator/EOF style delta across all 15 files beyond the excluded EP-style class. Both sides use LF-only line endings and have zero trailing-space lines (verified by grep).

findings.topic: Untrusted-FK statement shape (StockMovement FK_StockMovement_Supplier_SupplierId)
findings.v1: Emitted as a separate post-table batch: ALTER TABLE [dbo].[StockMovement]  WITH NOCHECK ADD CONSTRAINT ... (note stray double space after the table name), then ALTER ... NOCHECK CONSTRAINT
findings.v2: Emitted inline as a column-level constraint in CREATE TABLE, followed by ALTER ... NOCHECK CONSTRAINT and ALTER ... WITH NOCHECK CHECK CONSTRAINT batches
findings.difference: The trusted/disabled end-state semantics are the excluded NOCHECK class; what is new is the emission placement (separate WITH NOCHECK ADD batch vs inline definition + re-enable sequence) and v1's double-space artifact. Only instance in the estate.

findings.topic: Structural parity inventory (byte-identical emission across all 15 pairs)
findings.v1: Same as v2 for every listed feature
findings.v2: Same as v1 for every listed feature
findings.difference: Parity-confirmed classes: column ordering (both emit logical Order_Num order, identically diverging from physical order — physical Customer is ID,EMAIL,FIRSTNAME,LASTNAME,CITYID,LEGACYCODE); physical-only column LEGACYCODE dropped by both; physical INT identifiers widened to BIGINT by both; IDENTITY (1, 1) spelling; NVARCHAR/DECIMAL/VARBINARY/VARCHAR facet spellings; FILLFACTOR = 85 and STATISTICS_NORECOMPUTE = ON option text; filtered-index predicate WHERE ([Email] IS NOT NULL); ALTER INDEX [IX_Customer_LastName_FirstName] ... DISABLE; INCLUDE([WarehouseQty]); CREATE [UNIQUE] INDEX with no NONCLUSTERED/ASC keywords; IGNORE_DUP_KEY/DATA_COMPRESSION/PAD_INDEX absent from both; MS_Description values and coverage identical; BIT DEFAULT 1 rendering; CREATE TABLE bodies byte-identical for User, City, Country, CurrencyCode, Supplier, BillingAccount incl. UIX_BillingAccount_AccountNumber. User.sql's entire 27-line delta is the excluded Projection.* EP block.

findings.topic: Heap / allowMissingPrimaryKey handling — not exercisable in this estate
findings.v1: n/a — every exported entity has a PK (User is single-column PK-only, emitted identically)
findings.v2: n/a — same
findings.difference: No PK-less/heap table exists among the 15 live-estate entities, so heap emission and allowMissingPrimaryKey behavior cannot be compared from this evidence; any claim about that class needs the edge-case fixture (which v2 currently refuses: adapter.osm.indexFields).

findings.topic: (a) v2 refusal of model.edge-case.json — root cause
findings.v1: Osm.Json loader: IndexDocument.IsPrimary is a plain non-nullable bool (ModelJsonDeserializer.IndexDocument.cs:15-16) — absent property silently deserializes to false, then IndexDocumentMapper.cs:72 ORs it with kind=="PK" inference; isUnique likewise defaults false; all storage fields (isDisabled/fill_factor/dataSpace/partitionColumns/dataCompression) are nullable with defaults. The fixture loads without error or warning about these fields.
findings.v2: OssysJsonReader.parseIndex (sidecar/projection/src/Projection.Adapters.Osm/OssysJsonReader.fs:315-447) requires name via getString and isPrimary+isUnique via getBool; a missing property yields adapter.osm.missingProperty which collapses into the entity-level refusal adapter.osm.indexFields ('Required index fields missing on an entity in AppCore.City / ExtBilling.BillingAccount').
findings.difference: The two failing fixture entities are exactly the ones whose index JSON uses the minimal documented shape {name,isUnique,isPlatformAuto,columns} — missing only `isPrimary` (City OSIDX_CITY_NAME, BillingAccount IDX_BILLINGACCOUNT_ACCTNUM; Customer's and JobRun's fully-populated indexes parse fine). The repo's own contract says v2 is wrong: schema/cir-v1.json $defs/index.required=[name,columns] (isPrimary and isUnique optional), and readme.md §4's index example shows precisely the minimal shape with no isPrimary. Fix side: v2 — make isPrimary/isUnique optional-with-false-default (getOptionalBool already exists in OssysTranslation.fs).

findings.topic: (b) v2 refusal of v1 live-extracted model — index column channel (24 of 26 errors)
findings.v1: Extraction SQL writes columns[].attribute = HumanAttr = COALESCE(NULLIF(a.PhysicalColumnName,''), NULLIF(a.DatabaseColumnName,''), a.AttrName) (src/AdvancedSql/outsystems_metadata_rowsets.sql:590-635, emitted at :904) — physical name FIRST, so every one of the 21 indexes in model.extracted.json carries uppercase physical names ('ID','EMAIL','LASTNAME','ACCOUNTNUMBER'...) in `attribute`, while attribute records carry logical `name` ('Id','Email'...). v1's own consumer is insensitive: SmoIndexBuilder.cs:86 resolves column.Column.Value (physicalColumn) against BuildAttributeLookup keyed by LogicalName with StringComparer.OrdinalIgnoreCase (SmoEntityEmitter.cs:203-216) — works because OutSystems physical ≈ UPPER(logical) — and silently drops any index that fails to resolve.
findings.v2: parseIndex resolves key columns exclusively via getString col "attribute" → attributeSsKey(module, entity, attrName), producing Synthesized("OS_ATTR",[module;entity;"ID"]) which must structurally equal the attribute keys synthesized from logical `name` — SsKey is a plain F# DU (Identity.fs:80) so equality is exact/case-sensitive. Catalog.create's invariant walk (Projection.Core/Catalog.fs:2035) then emits catalog.index.danglingColumn for all 24 key columns across all 21 indexes (verified: 100% of indexes in the extracted model mismatch; physicalColumn is parsed but ignored for resolution).
findings.difference: Three stacked deltas: (1) v1's extractor writes the physical name into a field its own README §4 example ('attribute':'Email' vs 'physicalColumn':'EMAIL') and the field name 'HumanAttr' both imply should be logical — a v1 extractor deviation from its own contract; (2) v2 assumes `attribute` is the logical name (true only of hand-authored fixtures); (3) v2's match is case-sensitive and single-channel. Per contracts both sides bear a fix: v1's COALESCE order contradicts its README; but for interop the necessary change is v2's — resolve via `attribute` then fall back to `physicalColumn` matched case-insensitively against Attribute.Column.ColumnName (both are carried in the JSON).

findings.topic: (b) v2 refusal of v1 live-extracted model — cross-module references (2 of 26 errors)
findings.v1: The JSON carries refEntityId, refEntity_name AND refEntity_physicalName per reference attribute (e.g. Ops.JobRun.TriggeredByUserId → refEntityId 3001, refEntity_physicalName OSUSR_U_USER); v1's mapper (AttributeDocumentMapper.cs:119-160) keeps all three channels and resolves targets by physical table name across the whole model, so cross-module FKs work (live output emits FK_JobRun_User_TriggeredByUserId → [dbo].[User]).
findings.v2: OssysJsonReader.parseReference synthesizes TargetKind = kindSsKey sourceModuleName refEntityName (OssysJsonReader.fs:229) — the documented 'same-module assumption' (OssysTranslation.fs:552-556; DECISIONS.md:5345 rule 16, with an explicit re-open trigger 'until a fixture surfaces the case'). refEntityId and refEntity_physicalName are ignored. Result: Ops.JobRun→User synthesizes OS_KIND[Ops;User] and Sales.SalesOrder→Customer synthesizes OS_KIND[Sales;Customer], neither exists (User lives in SystemUsers, Customer in AppCore) → catalog.reference.danglingTarget (Catalog.fs:2017). Same-module refs (Customer→City, SalesOrder→Category) pass.
findings.difference: The rule-16 re-open trigger has now empirically fired: any real estate has cross-module FKs. v2's OWN rowset path already implements the correct rule — RefEntityId against a global kindKeysByEntityId map, then bundle-wide name fallback, then same-module degrade (OssysRowsetReader.fs:180-210) — which is why v2-live succeeded on the same estate. Fix side: v2's JSON path should mirror its rowset resolution (two-pass, bundle-wide by refEntity_physicalName or name).

findings.topic: (b) Is User special? Would supplementalModels.includeUsers=false have avoided it?
findings.v1: SystemUsers.User is a first-class module in the estate: espace 300 'SystemUsers' + entity 3001 'User'/OSUSR_U_USER are seeded rows in ossys-edge-case.seed.sql (lines 92/102), extracted like any module. supplementalModels.includeUsers governs a different mechanism entirely — injection of the built-in OutSystemsInternalModel.Users entity by SupplementalEntityLoader.cs (visible as the fx-lane-only Modules/UserExtension_CS/dbo.User.sql when the flag defaults true).
findings.v2: The PK_User_Id danglingColumn error on SystemUsers.User is one of 24 identical index-column failures (attribute 'ID' vs logical 'Id') — the same class as every other entity, nothing supplemental- or system-specific about it.
findings.difference: Verified: cfg.v1.live.json DID set supplementalModels.includeUsers=false, and SystemUsers.User still rode through because it is estate metadata, not supplemental injection. Even hypothetically excluding the module would leave 23 danglingColumn errors and both danglingTarget errors (the Ops.User synthesized key never matches anything regardless) — the refusal was unavoidable via that knob.

findings.topic: (c) Verdict: v2 file-mode as drop-in reader of v1 extraction outputs
findings.v1: v1 full-export consumes both its own live-extracted JSON and the hand fixture successfully (warn-and-proceed posture), producing complete out/ bundles in both lanes.
findings.v2: v2 file-mode refuses both artifact families: fixture → adapter.osm.indexFields (2 entities); live-extracted → 26 aggregated Catalog.create refusals (24 catalog.index.danglingColumn covering 100% of indexes + 2 catalog.reference.danglingTarget). v2 against the SAME estate via its rowset lane (model.ossys connection, v2-live/dist/onprem) succeeds fully — the incompatibility is exclusively in the SnapshotJson adapter.
findings.difference: NOT drop-in today. Refusal classes: (1) missing isPrimary/isUnique on contract-valid minimal index JSON; (2) physical-vs-logical + case-sensitive index-column identity; (3) same-module reference-target synthesis. Repo-contract disposition: V1_PARITY_MATRIX rows 23-28 mark the entire osm_model.json FOR-JSON aggregation ⚫ V1-SUNSET, row 26 explicitly calls today's JSON consumption 'indirect... until rowsets 10+11 lift' — the JSON lane is transitional by the repo's own contract. Either scope v1-extracted-JSON interop out explicitly (and say so in the refusal copy), or land the three v2 adapter fixes; optionally correct v1's HumanAttr COALESCE order to match its README.

findings.topic: (c) Latent silent divergence had parsing proceeded — IDENTITY loss
findings.v1: Live extraction emits zero `isAutoNumber` properties (0 occurrences in model.extracted.json) even though v1's own schema/cir-v1.json lists it in $defs/attribute.required — v1's CIR validator logs 306 best-effort warnings against v1's OWN artifact and proceeds; v1 recovers identity from the onDisk channel it lifts (onDisk.isIdentity=true; live output correctly emits IDENTITY(1,1)).
findings.v2: parseAttribute treats isAutoNumber as the sole IDENTITY source (IsIdentity = Ok true only; OssysJsonReader.fs:85-88) and by documented bound does not lift the onDisk sub-object at all (OssysJsonReader.fs:76-78) — so every live-extracted attribute would carry IsIdentity=false.
findings.difference: If the three refusal classes were fixed without also lifting onDisk.isIdentity (or v1 emitting isAutoNumber), v2 file-mode would silently emit all PK columns without IDENTITY — a schema-corrupting divergence with no diagnostic. Related same-family gaps: descriptions (live writes entity-level `meta` string + attribute-level `meta.description`; v2 reads only top-level `description` — 0 occurrences in either artifact → all MS_Description content dropped) and FK evidence (live writes attribute-level `hasDbConstraint`; v2 reads fixture-only `reference_hasDbConstraint` and defaults false, vs v1's tri-state null).

findings.topic: (c) Failure-mode posture: warn-and-proceed vs named refusal
findings.v1: v1 validates against cir-v1.json but proceeds best-effort on violations (9 issues on the fixture — meta-as-string, fill_factor; 306 on its own extraction), and its emitter silently DROPS an entire index when a column fails to resolve (SmoIndexBuilder.cs:86-90 break-and-skip) — an unnamed fidelity hazard.
findings.v2: v2 refuses at the boundary with named codes and stops before any output (adapter.osm.indexFields, catalog.index.danglingColumn, catalog.reference.danglingTarget), consistent with its 'every refusal named / downgrades never silent' law.
findings.difference: Philosophically v2's posture is the stronger contract (v1 can ship a bundle missing indexes without telling anyone). But two diagnosability gaps blunt it: adapter.osm.indexFields names only the entity, not the index nor the missing field (v1's validator names JSON-pointer paths), and the danglingColumn message doesn't hint that `physicalColumn` was present-but-ignored. Worth fixing message payloads whether or not the interop gaps close.

findings.topic: (d) v1-fx/out vs v1-live/out — artifact-class inventory
findings.v1: Both lanes emit the identical 13-kind artifact inventory: Bootstrap/AllEntitiesIncludingStatic.bootstrap.sql, Modules/<module>/<schema>.<table>.sql, Seeds/<module>/StaticEntities.seed.sql, OutSystemsModel.sqlproj, PostDeployment-Bootstrap.sql, full-export.manifest.json, manifest.json, opportunities.json, pipeline-telemetry.zip, policy-decision-report.json, policy-decisions.json, suggestions/{safe-to-apply,needs-remediation}.sql, validations.json (18 files fx vs 28 live — delta is entity census only). Per-table DDL uses the same emission classes in both: inline PK/named-or-bare DEFAULT/inline FK, CREATE [UNIQUE] INDEX with WHERE filter + WITH options, ALTER INDEX DISABLE, sp_addextendedproperty MS_Description blocks.
findings.v2: (comparison is v1-lane vs v1-lane; v2 not involved)
findings.difference: No fixture-lane-specific emission class exists — the fixture lane is structurally the same pipeline. All file-level differences trace to model/config content, enumerated in D2/D3.

findings.topic: (d) Fixture-lane-specific input provenance
findings.v1: fx lane: Seeds/AppCore/StaticEntities.seed.sql rows (Lisbon/Porto/Madrid for static City) come from the --static-data fixture tests/Fixtures/static-data/static-entities.edge-case.json (flag confirmed in batch2.sh; manifest static-seed stage scriptCount=1); profiler provider=Fixture reads profile.edge-case.json. fx also emits Modules/UserExtension_CS/dbo.User.sql — the built-in supplemental Users entity — because cfg.v1.fx.json omits supplementalModels and SupplementalModelOptions.Default has IncludeUsers=true.
findings.v2: (v1-lane comparison)
findings.difference: Live lane sources the same classes from the estate instead: Seeds/RefData/StaticEntities.seed.sql (2 MERGE blocks, Country + CurrencyCode — the estate's static entities) captured by the SQL profiler, and SystemUsers/dbo.User.sql from estate metadata with includeUsers=false suppressing the supplemental duplicate. Note the static-entity census itself differs between fixture (City static) and estate seed (RefData static, City not) — fixture and estate are intentionally non-identical models, so cross-lane output diffs conflate lane mechanics with model content.

findings.topic: (d) Within-DDL differences between lanes (same entity)
findings.v1: fx Customer.sql: bare DEFAULT (''), ON [FG_Customers] data-space clauses, IGNORE_DUP_KEY=ON on the filtered unique index, DATETIME for JobRun.CreatedOn, no FK on TriggeredByUserId, column order = fixture array order, BillingAccount under ExtBilling/billing schema.
findings.v2: (v1-lane comparison) live Customer.sql: NAMED default constraints (CONSTRAINT [DF_OSUSR_ABC_CUSTOMER_FIRSTNAME]...) from onDisk defaultConstraint evidence, no data-space clause and no IGNORE_DUP_KEY (the estate was seeded by v2's stripped seed — V1_PARITY_MATRIX row 2 divergence removed filegroups/partition scheme/IGNORE_DUP_KEY), DATETIME2 (deployed-storage refinement), FK_JobRun_User_TriggeredByUserId emitted (evidence-gated FK creation on clean orphan profile), ossys order-channel column ordering, BillingAccount under AppCore, inactive LegacyCode carried in model (isActive=false, physical_isPresentButInactive=true) but excluded from DDL.
findings.difference: Every delta is evidence-richness or model-content driven (onDisk reality channel, profile evidence, seed-stripping, module attribution), not a lane-mechanical emission difference — confirming the fixture lane exercises the same emitter surface with poorer evidence. Implication for parity work: v1-fx output is NOT a byte-comparison baseline for v1-live output; only class-level comparison is valid.

findings.topic: (d/c) v1's own extraction violates v1's own schema (best-effort warnings both lanes)
findings.v1: Both lanes log 'Schema validation encountered N issue(s). Proceeding with best-effort import' at every stage: fx N=9 (entity meta string-vs-object, fill_factor vs the schema's fillFactor spelling), live N=306 — every attribute in v1's OWN freshly-extracted model lacks the schema-required isAutoNumber. The loader tolerates both (nullable/defaulted DTOs; FillFactorNew fill_factor + FillFactorLegacy fillFactor dual mapping).
findings.v2: (context for interop) v2 has no equivalent lenient mode on the JSON lane; its strict reads collide with exactly these v1 self-inconsistencies.
findings.difference: The osm_model.json 'contract' is effectively defined by v1's tolerant loader, not by cir-v1.json or the README — three artifacts (README example, schema, live extractor output) mutually disagree on isPrimary presence, fill_factor naming, meta shape, hasDbConstraint naming, and attribute-vs-physical index columns. Any v2 interop fix should treat v1's loader behavior (defaults + multi-channel fallbacks + case-insensitive matching) as the de-facto spec, or the repo should first reconcile schema/README/extractor.

findings.topic: sqlproj format generation: legacy stub vs SDK-style
findings.v1: OutSystemsModel.sqlproj: legacy MSBuild-2003 namespace, DefaultTargets=Build, DSP Sql160, ModelVersion 2.0, ProjectVersion 4.2, TargetFrameworkVersion v4.7.2 — but NO <Import> of Microsoft.Data.Tools.Schema.SqlTasks.targets, no ProjectGuid, no Configuration/Platform, no OutputType (BuildSsdtSqlProjectStep.cs:110-145 writes only PropertyGroup + item groups)
findings.v2: ProjectionCatalog.sqlproj: SDK-style <Project Sdk="Microsoft.Build.Sql/2.2.0">, Name + DSP Sql160 + ModelCollation only; relies on SDK default **/*.sql Build glob; XmlWriter-emitted, trailing newline, forward-slash paths (SqlprojEmitter.fs)
findings.difference: V1 mimics a legacy SSDT project but omits the parts that make one loadable/buildable; V2 emits the current Microsoft-documented SDK project shape, which its own gated tests dotnet-build to a .dacpac

findings.topic: sqlproj item inventory
findings.v1: 15 explicit Build Include items (backslash paths, enumerated from disk OrdinalIgnoreCase-sorted); PostDeploy Include=Seeds\**\*.sql glob; Bootstrap/ and PostDeployment-Bootstrap.sql are NOT project items at all (dangling files); no None items, no refactorlog
findings.v2: Implicit SDK Build glob picks up Modules/** and Schemas/billing.sql; Build Remove + PostDeploy Include Script.PostDeployment.sql; Build Remove + None Include for Data/Bootstrap.sql and Data/StaticSeeds.sql; RefactorLog Include ProjectionCatalog.refactorlog — exactly the Remove+None pattern Microsoft Learn's pre/post-deployment-scripts doc prescribes
findings.difference: V2's item algebra is closed (every emitted .sql is classified except manifest.remediation.sql — see C1); V1 leaves its bootstrap artifacts entirely outside the project and only V2 emits CREATE SCHEMA (Schemas/billing.sql) for the build glob to include

findings.topic: collation pins (project-level and column-level)
findings.v1: ModelCollation 1033,CI plus DefaultCollation SQL_Latin1_General_CP1_CI_AS at project level; but drops per-column COLLATE from DDL — deployed Customer.Email came out SQL_Latin1_General_CP1_CI_AS (roundtrip catalog.diff)
findings.v2: ModelCollation "1033, CI" only (no DefaultCollation property); preserves per-column COLLATE in DDL — deployed Customer.Email is Latin1_General_CI_AI, matching the source estate
findings.difference: Project-level pins are near-equivalent (v1's extra DefaultCollation is a build-model default, cosmetic here); the operative divergence is column-level: v2 carries source column collations through, v1 silently loses them, so v1's project-level pin papers over a real fidelity gap

findings.topic: v1 'PostDeploy Include Seeds\**\*.sql' vs the one-post-deploy-script rule
findings.v1: The glob expands at MSBuild evaluation to every seed file under Seeds/. This run: exactly 1 file (Seeds/RefData/StaticEntities.seed.sql) so the project is legal. But BuildSsdtStaticSeedStep.cs:103-147 writes one file PER MODULE with static entities plus an optional master StaticEntities.seed.sql (EmitMasterFile) — any multi-module-static estate yields N>1 PostDeploy items
findings.v2: Exactly one PostDeploy item (Script.PostDeployment.sql) which :r-includes the lane files; the SDK inlines them into the dacpac's single post-deploy at build — the documented pattern for multiple scripts
findings.difference: Microsoft Learn (sql-database-projects/concepts/pre-post-deployment-scripts): 'A SQL project file can have a single pre-deployment script and a single post-deployment script specified'; multiple files must go through one :r-including script with the extras Build-Removed. V1's glob violates this on any estate with >1 seed file — VS refuses a second PostDeploy mark and DacFx-based builds fail or take only one file, silently dropping the rest from the publish; with EmitMasterFile the master also DUPLICATES the module seeds. Latent this run only because the estate had one static module

findings.topic: v1 sqlproj step ordering: seed enumeration is dead code, output is history-dependent
findings.v1: BuildSsdtPipeline.cs:88-90 runs _sqlProjectStep BEFORE _staticSeedStep, so BuildSsdtSqlProjectStep.cs:45 enumerates a Seeds/ dir that never exists yet on a clean run — the per-file PostDeploy enumeration branch (lines 170-176) is unreachable and the glob fallback always fires. On a DIRTY re-run into the same out dir (v1 has no atomic-replace), stale prior-run Seeds/ and Modules/ files get enumerated into the project, so the sqlproj content depends on directory history
findings.v2: Sqlproj derives from outputs.DataBundle (the in-memory lane map, Pipeline.fs:1640-1643), 'derived from the ACTUAL data-lane file set so the post-deploy :r includes can never dangle'; whole bundle written via atomic staging replace (Pipeline.fs writeWith), so no stale-file capture is possible; v2-live-proj differs from v2-live by exactly {ProjectionCatalog.sqlproj, Script.PostDeployment.sql}
findings.difference: V1's project synthesis reads the filesystem mid-pipeline at the wrong time; V2's derives from the typed outputs and is additive and deterministic (v2-determinism.diff = 0 lines; v1's sqlproj was deterministic across the two clean-dir runs but is dirty-dir sensitive by code-read)

findings.topic: bootstrap wiring: v1 copy-me template vs v2 None'd separate step
findings.v1: PostDeployment-Bootstrap.sql written at out root with '-- Usage: Copy this file to your SSDT project's PostDeployment folder'; not a project item; its :r paths (Bootstrap\..., Seeds\...) are out-root-relative and would break if the file is copied into a PostDeployment subfolder as instructed; copying it in as the post-deploy would also collide with the existing PostDeploy glob (second post-deploy) and re-apply the same seeds the glob applies
findings.v2: Data/Bootstrap.sql is Build-Removed and None'd in the sqlproj and deliberately NOT :r-included by Script.PostDeployment.sql — the header comment and PostDeployEmitter.fs doc state the Bootstrap MERGE lane is the separate post-publish data-load step the operator runs (operator decision 2026-06-24)
findings.difference: V2 makes the bootstrap's out-of-band status machine-enforced (None item) and self-documenting; V1's manual copy-me instruction is self-contradictory (breaks its own :r paths, double-wires seeds, violates the single post-deploy rule if followed literally)

findings.topic: refactorlog item (v2 only)
findings.v1: No refactorlog anywhere — physical→logical renames are unrepresented, so any incremental DacFx publish over a prior deployment treats renames as DROP+CREATE
findings.v2: ProjectionCatalog.refactorlog emitted in BOTH modes (store-threaded G3 run), explicit <RefactorLog Include> item so dotnet build embeds refactor.xml (gated build test proves the pairing); on this run the file is an empty <Operations/> (no prior-chain displacement) but the wiring is live
findings.difference: V2 carries the rename-provenance channel SSDT needs for sp_rename-instead-of-DROP on incremental publish; V1 has no equivalent surface at all

findings.topic: would v1's sqlproj build? (assessed, not executed)
findings.v1: No, nowhere, as-is: (1) legacy format needs Windows VS SSDT's Microsoft.Data.Tools.Schema.SqlTasks.targets and the emitted file contains NO Import element, so even on a full SSDT box plain msbuild has no Build target (MSB4057) and dotnet build cannot parse 2003-format projects; (2) the repo ships zero SSDT/DacFx/msbuild-sql support for the C# root (grep: all Microsoft.Build.Sql/SqlTasks references live in the sidecar); (3) even after hand-adding the Import + Configuration scaffolding, model validation fails: billing.BillingAccount.sql references schema [billing] and v1 never emits CREATE SCHEMA (roundtrip had to hand-create it — logged 'V1 GAP: not emitted'), an SQL71501 unresolved-reference build error; (4) the JobRun file's trailing ALTER TABLE ... DISABLE TRIGGER [TR_OSUSR_XYZ_JOBRUN_AUDIT] names a trigger that does not exist in the model (v1 renamed the trigger to TR_JobRun_AUDIT but not the DISABLE reference)
findings.v2: n/a (see B2)
findings.difference: V1's sqlproj is an import-fodder stub for the docs' 'open in SSDT or copy contents into an existing project' flow, not a buildable project; its own docs hedge accordingly

findings.topic: would v2's sqlproj build? (assessed, not executed)
findings.v1: n/a (see B1)
findings.v2: Yes in mechanism, with two gates: (1) the Microsoft.Build.Sql/2.2.0 SDK must be NuGet-restorable — the bundle ships NO nuget.config (packet G2); the repo's build tests write their own nuget.org-pinned nuget.config before dotnet build and soft-skip when the SDK is unrestorable, and the emitter header records 'verified buildable 2026-06-24' only WITH a nuget.config; (2) on THIS estate the build would fail model validation anyway: Modules/Ops/dbo.JobRun.sql:77-78 and StockMovement's triggers CREATE TRIGGER ... ON [dbo].[OSUSR_XYZ_JOBRUN]/[OSUSR_INV_MOVEMENT] — physical names that resolve to nothing in the model (the same dangling references that failed the manual deploy with Msg 8197), i.e. SQL71501 unresolved-reference errors
findings.difference: V2's build path is real and cross-platform (proven by gated tests on synthetic catalogs) but the shipped ParityEstate bundle would not compile until the trigger-rename defect (a sibling-packet finding) is fixed, and no operator can build offline without supplying a feed config

findings.topic: G5 hazard #1: manifest.remediation.sql at bundle root, not Build-Removed — CONFIRMED on current tree
findings.v1: n/a (v1 has no SDK glob; its remediation equivalents live under suggestions/ and are not project items)
findings.v2: Confirmed still present at ea21b13: Pipeline.fs:1036-1037 writes ArtifactPath.remediation='manifest.remediation.sql' unconditionally into the bundle root (no emission flag); SqlprojEmitter.fs:89-104 Build-Removes only PostDeployEmitter.fileName + the Data/ lanes; both v2-live and v2-live-proj listings show the file at root, byte-identical. This run it is comment-only ('No remediation candidates surfaced') so the SDK glob compiles an empty model contribution — benign. But RemediationEmitter emits ACTIVE top-level 'SELECT * FROM ... WHERE ...;' statements ('OPTION 1 (SELECT) is active') whenever operator-attention findings exist, and a Build-classified .sql containing bare SELECTs fails DacFx model build ('statement not recognized in this context')
findings.difference: Any estate with ≥1 remediation finding produces a bundle whose own sqlproj cannot build; the gated real-publish build test doesn't catch it because its tiny model yields a comment-only remediation file. Fix is one line in the emitted ItemGroup (Build Remove + None, same as the lanes)

findings.topic: G5 hazard #2: post-deploy :r order alphabetical when both StaticSeeds and MigrationData exist — CONFIRMED on current tree
findings.v1: n/a (v1's post-deploy carries only seeds; its bootstrap phasing is a different lane)
findings.v2: Confirmed at ea21b13: Pipeline.fs:1640-1646 takes outputs.DataBundle Map keys (key-sorted) |> List.sort, filters out Data/Bootstrap.sql, and hands the remainder to PostDeployEmitter.renderIncludes which preserves caller order — so with both lanes present the emitted script reads ':r Data/MigrationData.sql' BEFORE ':r Data/StaticSeeds.sql'. That inverts the composer's semantic lane order (DataEmissionComposer.fs:402-405 lists StaticSeeds, MigrationData, Bootstrap) and the post-deploy header's own promise ('static-entity seeds + migration dependency rows'). This run had no MigrationData, so the emitted file shows only StaticSeeds and the hazard is dormant
findings.difference: Operator-published migration rows that FK-reference static-entity rows (the typical shape — lookups are exactly what static entities are) would MERGE before their parents exist and fail the publish at post-deploy time; no test pins the two-lane ordering (SqlprojWireTests covers presence only; the real-publish build test runs MigrationData-only)

findings.topic: v1 PostDeployment-Bootstrap.sql physical-name guard — full classification
findings.v1: Four independent defects, one proven live: (1) NAME-PLANE SPLIT (proven): the guard 'IF NOT EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM [dbo].[OSUSR_REF_COUNTRY])' is built from canonicalTable.Definition.PhysicalName (BuildSsdtPostDeploymentTemplateStep.cs:96-100) while the same bundle's DDL deploys logical dbo.Country — the guard batch dies with invalid-object error 208 on the very database the bundle creates, and the ENTIRE bootstrap payload it wraps is also physical-named ([dbo].[OSUSR_DEF_CITY] etc.): roundtrip v1-deploy.log FAIL, 'Msg 1088 Cannot find the object dbo.OSUSR_DEF_CITY'. (2) BATCH-STRUCTURE: ':r Bootstrap\AllEntitiesIncludingStatic.bootstrap.sql' sits inside IF...BEGIN, but sqlcmd/SSDT inline :r textually and the included file contains 12 GO separators — the IF block would be shattered into invalid batches even with correct names. (3) SEMANTIC FRAGILITY: 'first deployment' is proxied by emptiness of an arbitrary first static table, so truncating that one table re-applies the whole 4-entity snapshot. (4) NONDETERMINISM: DateTime.UtcNow header (line 85) makes it one of v1's 10 nondeterministic files (v1-determinism.diff), alongside the bootstrap snapshot itself. Compounding: the file is not a project item and its copy-me instruction breaks its own relative :r paths (A6)
findings.v2: No guard construct at all: bootstrap is an explicitly manual, unconditional post-publish MERGE lane (idempotent by MERGE semantics), None'd out of the build
findings.difference: V1's guard is unexecutable-by-construction against v1's own deployed schema — the bootstrap lane can never load through the documented template; v2 avoids the guard problem structurally but its bootstrap has its own ordering failure (E2)

findings.topic: v1 supported deploy contract to an empty DB, and whether it works
findings.v1: Contract (docs/full-export-artifact-contract.md 'SSDT Integration Playbook' + line 338: 'the recommended flow is: publish the DACPAC (which runs static seeds via post-deployment), execute the dynamic data replay...'): open/import the sqlproj in SSDT, hand-create a Script.PostDeployment.sql with :r includes of the seeds (the docs instruct manual assembly DESPITE the emitted PostDeploy glob), publish; bootstrap = copy-me template. Empirically (roundtrip, sqlcmd emulation in hand-computed topo order): all 15 DDL files OK but ONLY after hand-creating the billing schema (v1 emits no CREATE SCHEMA); Seeds/StaticEntities.seed.sql OK (logical-named, idempotent MERGE); Bootstrap lane FAIL (physical names, D1). The dacpac path itself is unreachable (B1)
findings.v2: n/a (see E2)
findings.difference: V1's supported path has three hand-steps not in any artifact (create schema, assemble post-deploy, repair/skip bootstrap) and its only automated deploy surface (the sqlproj) doesn't build; deployed result is schema+static-seeds only, no bootstrap data

findings.topic: v2 supported deploy contract to an empty DB, and whether it works
findings.v1: n/a (see E1)
findings.v2: Contract (SqlprojEmitter.fs/PostDeployEmitter.fs doc headers + DECISIONS 2026-06-24): dotnet build ProjectionCatalog.sqlproj → sqlpackage /Action:Publish (schema + inlined StaticSeeds post-deploy) → operator runs Data/Bootstrap.sql against the live DB; manifest.json additionally publishes deploymentBatches (H-038, 'FK-safe parallel deployment batches') for manual/parallel deploys. Empirically (roundtrip, sqlcmd emulation): Schemas/billing.sql OK, 13/15 DDL OK, JobRun + StockMovement FAIL (physical-named trigger targets, Msg 8197); Data/StaticSeeds.sql OK; Data/Bootstrap.sql FAIL — Msg 547 FK_Customer_City_CityId because the lane MERGEs Customer BEFORE City. Root cause visible in the manifest itself: deploymentBatches on this estate is 15 SINGLETON batches in SsKey/declaration order (Customer #1, City #2) — TopologicalOrder.levels' honest degenerate for non-Topological mode (TopologicalOrder.fs:313-317) — so the published batch order is NOT FK-safe and the same order drives the Bootstrap lane rendering
findings.difference: V2's contract is the better-specified one (machine-readable batches, documented lane semantics, buildable project format) but on this estate the schema publish would fail at build (triggers), and the manual path fails the bootstrap lane on FK order; the manifest's 'FK-safe' doc-comment promise is not delivered when the topo pass degenerates, and nothing in the manifest flags the degenerate mode to the operator

findings.topic: overall deploy-order-contract verdict
findings.v1: Empty-DB deploy reaches schema + static seeds only via undocumented hand-steps; bootstrap data unreachable (wrong name plane); sqlproj unbuildable; contract is prose in docs/full-export-artifact-contract.md that contradicts the emitted artifacts (tells users to hand-write the post-deploy the sqlproj already globs)
findings.v2: Empty-DB deploy reaches schema (minus 2 trigger files) + static seeds via artifacts that agree with each other and with Microsoft's documented SDK pattern; bootstrap data unreachable this estate (lane order not FK-safe); two latent build-breakers (C1, C2) sit in the sqlproj mode; bundle is byte-deterministic and the sqlproj flag is purely additive (v2-live vs v2-live-proj differ by exactly the 2 project files)
findings.difference: Neither side's bundle deploys data-complete to an empty database as shipped. V2's failures are point defects inside a coherent, testable contract (trigger rename, topo degeneracy, two one-line sqlproj emitter gaps); V1's failures are contract-level: the deploy story spans four artifacts (sqlproj glob, copy-me template, physical-named bootstrap, absent schema DDL) that cannot all be followed simultaneously

findings.topic: Console channel architecture
findings.v1: Single merged text stream (v1-live/console.log, 243 lines) interleaving three registers: MEL 'info:' logger lines, 'Pipeline execution log:' pipe-delimited key=value telemetry, and human summary sections; no run identifier; no machine-readable channel.
findings.v2: Dual channel: human answer on stdout (31 lines: 28 artifacts with byte sizes + 'This run recorded — episode 1 on timeline DEV'); typed NDJSON envelopes on stderr (46 events, every one carrying runId ULID, ts, level, category, code, phase, stepId, payload).
findings.difference: v2 gives a parseable, correlatable event stream plus a terse human answer; v1 gives one grep-only text blob with no run identity.

findings.topic: Warning visibility on the human success channel
findings.v1: Warnings printed inline in the console summary (schema-validation block, FK anomaly table with '⚠️', 'ATTENTION' callouts in v1-fx.console.log) — impossible to miss, albeit duplicated.
findings.v2: In the captured --json/pipe mode, stdout.log contains zero warning text on success; the 44 model-read notices (28 storageDivergence + 15 primaryKey.divergence warnings) and 3 structural.cycleUnresolved warns exist only as stderr NDJSON events and the notices artifact. Exit code 0.
findings.difference: An operator reading v2's stdout (or a CI step capturing only stdout) sees a clean run while 43 warning-severity findings and unresolvable FK cycles went to stderr; v1 surfaced equivalents inline. (v2's TTY 'verdict panel' mode is documented in usage but not captured empirically.)

findings.topic: Warning fidelity, rollup and persistence
findings.v1: 306 schema-validation issues capped at 3 examples + '… 303 additional issue(s) suppressed.'; the suppressed 303 are persisted nowhere (console, manifest and artifacts all carry only the same 5 lines); examples cite JSON pointers (/modules/0/entities/0/attributes/0), not entity names; the identical block is rendered ~6 times in one console run.
findings.v2: One warn event adapter.ossys.modelRead.noticeRollup with category counts (nullability:1, primaryKey:15, other:28, total:44), inline samples naming columns (DISPLAYLABEL, ID, CITYID) and engine policy ('The engine carries the LOGICAL value'), plus artifactPath to notices/model-read/<runId>.json itemizing all 44 with codes, severities and metadata.
findings.difference: v2 preserves 100% of warning detail on disk and compresses the stream to one rollup; v1 permanently discards 99% of instances while repeating the summary 6x.

findings.topic: Failure surfacing contract (refusals)
findings.v1: No comparable refusal on record; on the same edge-case-class inputs v1 proceeds best-effort with warnings and exit 0; source shows failures write free-text errors and default exit 1 (PipelineCommandFactory.GetFailureExitCode => 1), no error codes.
findings.v2: Both refusal runs (v2-fx-edge, v2-from-v1model) exit 2 with machine codes (adapter.osm.indexFields; catalog.index.danglingColumn/catalog.reference.danglingTarget), per-object messages naming module.entity/index/column, error-level NDJSON events, a hero line 'Stopped before any change was applied. The cause is shown below.' — verified accurate (no dist/onprem was created) — and an action line '→ Correct the model and rerun.'
findings.difference: v2 refusals are atomic (no partial outputs), named, and machine-actionable; v1 either continues on bad input or fails with untyped text and exit 1.

findings.topic: Refusal render defects (human block)
findings.v1: Error text, when printed, is complete (no width truncation observed).
findings.v2: Human problem lines are truncated at console width with '…' even when piped to a file (e.g. dacpac run: 'DacFx emission failed: ... Error…' — the actual SQL71001 text only in NDJSON); messages leak F# internals ('Synthesized ("OS_IDX", ["AppCore"; ...])'); the v2-from-v1model block lists 26 problems sequentially with no dedup/rollup (24x the same code), contradicting v2's own noticeRollup pattern; no hint that the rejected file is a v1-format model.
findings.difference: v2's failure panel loses load-bearing detail to truncation and internal-representation noise exactly when the operator needs it; full text requires parsing the NDJSON.

findings.topic: Exit code taxonomy
findings.v1: Undocumented binary: 0 success (including 306 schema issues and orphan-FK data findings), 1 on failure (dmm-compare alone returns 2); no published contract.
findings.v2: Published 0–9 axis taxonomy in CLI usage (0 ok, 1 argv, 2 parse, 3 execution, 6 config/connection, 7 gate/permission, 9 fail-loud, 5/8 divergence axes) plus a single total classifier (CliExit.fs) replacing six drifted per-verb ladders; empirical: refusals=2, successes=0.
findings.difference: v2 exit codes are a documented operator API enabling scripted triage; v1's convey only pass/fail.

findings.topic: Run-manifest machine contract
findings.v1: full-export.manifest.json: 4 stages with timestamps/Duration/Warnings/artifact name→path maps + DynamicArtifacts[12] with contentType. Defects: no outcome/status field, no counts, no run id, extract-model StartedAtUtc=null, static-seed Duration is a copy of build-ssdt's span, warnings duplicated (extract block twice; same block in 4 places), absolute machine-specific paths.
findings.v2: summary.runComplete envelope: outcome per stage + durationMs (extract 2336, profile 33, emit 479, pipeline 2859, store 31), 29 artifacts with byte sizes, eventCounts by level, transformSummary (registered 45/applied 0), rationaleHistogram, suggestedConfigEdits, 245 bench aggregates; all keyed by runId and mirrored to bench/full-export/<runId>.json (231 labeled perf stats).
findings.difference: v2's run record answers 'did it work, how long, what was produced, what fired' in typed form; v1's records only when and where, with internal inconsistencies.

findings.topic: Cross-run provenance (episode ledger)
findings.v1: None: full-export.manifest.json is overwritten per run, is nondeterministic, and carries no version/lineage concept.
findings.v2: lifecycle/onprem.json episode ledger: timeline DEV, coordinate (versionOrdinal 0/versionLabel v0/environment/at), full schema snapshot (codecVersion 2, 7 modules), 78 appliedTransforms, CDC counters, tolerances; stdout announces 'episode 1 on timeline DEV; 0 refactorlog entr(ies) accumulated'; bundle ships ProjectionCatalog.refactorlog; diff/compare/explain verbs operate over refs including @<runId>. Gap: the episode carries no runId, so ledger↔run.ndjson/bench/notices correlation is by timestamp only.
findings.difference: v2 makes 'what changed since last publish' a first-class, announced concept; v1 has no cross-run story at all.

findings.topic: Artifact inventory presentation
findings.v1: Artifacts printed inline with role annotations ('Decision log:', 'Safe script:', 'Opportunities:', 'Run manifest:', seed/telemetry lists) as absolute paths; no sizes.
findings.v2: Flat listing of 28 relative paths with byte sizes, no role annotation; roles are inferable only from filenames (fidelity.txt, manifest.summary.txt) or the bundle manifest.
findings.difference: v2 gains sizes and determinism but loses v1's guided 'this file is for X' narrative — the operator's what-should-I-look-at map.

findings.topic: Human summary content on success (stage detail, policy echo, apply status)
findings.v1: Console prints per-module tightening summaries, 'Tightening Statistics' (41/56 NOT NULL, 18/18 UNIQUE, 8/8 FK), 16 tightening toggles with provenance '(Default)', narrative rationale sentences, SQL validation '15/15 validated, 0 errors', and an explicit 'Schema apply: skipped' section listing the skipped scripts.
findings.v2: stdout has none of this; partial machine equivalents exist (bundle manifest coverage 100%, manifest.summary.txt, fidelity.txt, rationaleHistogram) but nothing on the success console states stage stats, policy configuration, validation, or that no database was touched ('episode recorded' could even suggest a deploy happened).
findings.difference: The at-a-glance human accounting of what the policy did and what was not applied is a v1 console feature with no v2 console equivalent in the captured mode.

findings.topic: Progress reporting
findings.v1: No progress ticks; only stage begin/end log lines with inline millisecond timings.
findings.v2: 23 summary.stageProgress NDJSON events with done/total/elapsedMs during extract; usage documents a live TTY stage board (AUTO channel) — not captured empirically.
findings.difference: v2 exposes machine-consumable progress within stages; v1 is silent between stage boundaries.

findings.topic: NDJSON stream purity on failure
findings.v1: n/a (no NDJSON channel).
findings.v2: On refusal, the human error panel is written to stderr after the summary.runComplete event, so the captured run.ndjson contains 8–55 raw non-JSON lines (v2-fx-edge: 8; v2-from-v1model: 55); a strict line-by-line JSON consumer breaks unless it tolerates raw text.
findings.difference: v2's machine channel is contaminated by its own human failure render in pipe mode; success streams are 100% parseable.

findings.topic: Predictive accuracy of publish-time warnings (bootstrap FK cycles)
findings.v1: Console asserted 'bootstrap.snapshot.generated ... ordering.cycleDetected=false' with no warning; its Bootstrap script later failed at deploy (roundtrip: 'FAIL Bootstrap/AllEntitiesIncludingStatic.bootstrap.sql', missing-object errors).
findings.v2: Emitted 3 structural.cycleUnresolved warns at publish (one auto-resolved via weak-FK deferral, two 'unresolvable: ... every edge non-nullable or cascade; make one of its FK columns nullable...'); its Data/Bootstrap.sql later failed at deploy with exactly an FK-ordering conflict (FK_Customer_City_CityId).
findings.difference: v2 pre-announced the failure class with remediation advice (though only in stderr NDJSON and naming entities by opaque ssKey 'bbbbbbbb...'); v1 claimed the ordering was clean.

findings.topic: Exit-code binning of dacpac emission failure
findings.v1: n/a.
findings.v2: First v2-live-proj attempt (emission.dacpac=true) failed with error event emitter.dacpac.failed (DacFx SQL71001) and exited 2, which the published usage taxonomy labels 'parse error (model JSON / spec / config-parse)' — an emit/execution failure axis (1 or 3) would fit better; the human panel truncated the DacFx error to 'Error…'.
findings.difference: One observed v2 failure lands on an arguably wrong published axis, weakening the taxonomy's triage value for this case.

findings.topic: Determinism of console output and run records
findings.v1: Rerun produced 1965-line diff across 10 out/ files (both manifests, policy logs, opportunities, validations, scripts) due to embedded timestamps; console differs likewise.
findings.v2: Rerun byte-identical: v2-determinism.diff is 0 lines and stdout.log is identical across runs (verified); all volatile data (ts, runId) is confined to the NDJSON stream and runId-named side artifacts.
findings.difference: v2 bundles and human output are directly diffable run-over-run — an operational 'what changed' primitive v1 lacks.

findings.topic: Decision-summary content parity (bundle digest vs v1 stats)
findings.v1: Console and artifacts report a populated decision plane: 41/56 columns tightened, 18/18 unique, 8/8 FKs, 65 validations, per-module breakdowns.
findings.v2: dist/onprem/manifest.summary.txt reports all-zero decisions ('Totals: 0 structural tightening(s); 0 remediation finding(s)') and runComplete transformSummary shows applied:0/registered:45 on the same estate; rationaleHistogram is empty.
findings.difference: v2's human decision digest is empty where v1's is rich — either the digest is not wired to v2's decision plane or tightening semantics moved elsewhere (fidelity/notices); as an operator report it currently answers 'what did policy decide' with zeros.

findings.topic: Warnings vs exit code (success-with-warnings)
findings.v1: Exit 0 despite 306 schema issues and orphan-FK ATTENTION findings.
findings.v2: Exit 0 despite 44 notices (43 warning-severity) and 2 unresolvable FK-cycle warns.
findings.difference: Both sides treat warnings as non-fatal and reserve nonzero exits for hard failures; neither offers a strict/warnings-as-errors mode in the captured runs.
